The land which became the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") was inhabited by [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") for tens of thousands of years; their descendants include but may not be limited to 574 federally recognized tribes. The history of the present-day United States began in 1607 with the establishment of [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia") in modern-day Virginia by settlers who arrived from the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), and the landing of the *[Mayflower](/wiki/Mayflower "Mayflower")* by English pilgrims to [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth,_Massachusetts "Plymouth, Massachusetts") in 1620. In the late 15th century, [European colonization](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas") began and largely [decimated](/wiki/Native_American_disease_and_epidemics "Native American disease and epidemics") [Indigenous societies](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") through wars and epidemics. By the 1760s, the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies"), then part of [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America") and by extension the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), were established. The [Southern Colonies](/wiki/Southern_Colonies "Southern Colonies") built an agricultural system on [slave labor](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States") and [enslaving millions from Africa](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade"). After the British victory over the [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") in the [French and Indian Wars](/wiki/French_and_Indian_Wars "French and Indian Wars"), [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") imposed a series of taxes and issued the [Intolerable Acts](/wiki/Intolerable_Acts "Intolerable Acts") on the colonies in 1773, which were designed to end self-governance. Tensions between the colonies and British authorities subsequently intensified, leading to the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), which commenced with the [Battles of Lexington and Concord](/wiki/Battles_of_Lexington_and_Concord "Battles of Lexington and Concord") on April 19, 1775. In June 1775, the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") established the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") and unanimously selected [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") as its commander-in-chief. The following year, on July 4, 1776, the Second Continental Congress unanimously declared its independence, issuing the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"). On September 3, 1783, in the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), the British acknowledged the independence and sovereignty of the Thirteen Colonies, leading to the establishment of the United States. On September 17, 1787, the [U.S. Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States") was signed by a [majority of delegates](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States"), and was later ratified by the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies"), leading to the first modern U.S. government.

In the [1788-89 presidential election](/wiki/1788%E2%80%9389_United_States_presidential_election "1788–89 United States presidential election"), Washington was elected the nation's first [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"). Along with his [Treasury Secretary](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury"), [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"), Washington sought to create a relatively stronger central government than that favored by other [founders](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States"), including [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") and [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"). On March 4, 1789, the new nation [debated, adopted, and ratified](/wiki/Constitutional_Convention_(United_States) "Constitutional Convention (United States)") the [U.S. Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States"), which is now the oldest and longest-standing written and codified national constitution in the world.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In 1791, a [Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights") was added to guarantee [inalienable rights](/wiki/Natural_rights_and_legal_rights "Natural rights and legal rights"). In 1803, Jefferson, then serving as the [nation's third president](/wiki/Presidency_of_Thomas_Jefferson "Presidency of Thomas Jefferson"), negotiated the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase"), which doubled the size of the country. Encouraged by available, inexpensive land, and the notion of [manifest destiny](/wiki/Manifest_destiny "Manifest destiny"), the country underwent [westward expansion](/wiki/Westward_expansion "Westward expansion") in a project of [settler colonialism](/wiki/Settler_colonialism "Settler colonialism") marked by a [series of conflicts](/wiki/Indian_Wars "Indian Wars") with the continent's [indigenous inhabitants](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"). The most notable advocate of [manifest destiny](/wiki/Manifest_destiny "Manifest destiny") was President [James K. Polk](/wiki/James_K._Polk "James K. Polk"), who annexed [Texas](/wiki/Republic_of_Texas "Republic of Texas") in 1845, and declared war on [Mexico](/wiki/Second_Federal_Republic_of_Mexico "Second Federal Republic of Mexico") the next year. An overwhelming U.S. victory in the [Mexican-American War](/wiki/Mexican-American_War "Mexican-American War") led to the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") in 1848, where the U.S. acquired much of the [American Southwest](/wiki/American_Southwest "American Southwest") from Mexico. Whether or not slavery should be legal in the expanded territories was an issue of national contention, and led to increasing tensions over [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery").

Following the election of [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") as the nation's 16th president in the [1860 presidential election](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election "1860 United States presidential election"), southern states [seceded](/wiki/Ordinance_of_Secession "Ordinance of Secession") and formed the pro-slavery [Confederate States of America](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America"). In April 1861, at the [Battle of Fort Sumter](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Sumter "Battle of Fort Sumter"), Confederates launched the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). However, the [Union](/wiki/Union_army "Union army")'s victory at the [Battle of Gettysburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Gettysburg "Battle of Gettysburg"), the [deadliest battle in American military history](/wiki/List_of_battles_with_most_United_States_military_fatalities "List of battles with most United States military fatalities") with over 50,000 casualties, proved a [turning point in the war](/wiki/Turning_point_of_the_American_Civil_War "Turning point of the American Civil War"), leading to the [Union's victory](/wiki/Conclusion_of_the_American_Civil_War "Conclusion of the American Civil War") in 1865, which preserved the nation. On April 15, 1865, Lincoln was [assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Assassination of Abraham Lincoln"). The Confederates' defeat led to the [abolition of slavery](/wiki/Thirteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution"). In the subsequent [Reconstruction era](/wiki/Reconstruction_era "Reconstruction era") from 1865 to 1877, the national government [gained explicit duty to protect individual rights](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution"). In 1877, white southern Democrats regained political power in the South, often using paramilitary [suppression of voting](/wiki/Voter_suppression_in_the_United_States "Voter suppression in the United States") and [Jim Crow laws](/wiki/Jim_Crow_laws "Jim Crow laws") to maintain [white supremacy](/wiki/White_supremacy "White supremacy"). During the [Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age") from the late 19th century to the early 20th century, the United States emerged as the world's leading industrial power, largely due to entrepreneurship, [industrialization](/wiki/Second_Industrial_Revolution "Second Industrial Revolution"), and the [arrival of millions of immigrant workers](/wiki/European_emigration "European emigration"). Dissatisfaction with corruption, inefficiency, and traditional politics stimulated the [Progressive movement](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era"), leading to reforms, including to the [federal income tax](/wiki/Income_tax_in_the_United_States "Income tax in the United States"), direct election of [U.S. Senators](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"), citizenship for many Indigenous people, [alcohol prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States"), and [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_the_United_States "Women's suffrage in the United States").

Initially neutral during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the United States [declared war on Germany](/wiki/American_entry_into_World_War_I "American entry into World War I") in 1917, joining the successful [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"). After the prosperous [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties"), the [Wall Street crash of 1929](/wiki/Wall_Street_crash_of_1929 "Wall Street crash of 1929") marked the onset of a decade-long global [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_States "Great Depression in the United States"). President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") launched [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") programs, including [unemployment relief](/wiki/Unemployment_benefits "Unemployment benefits") and [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Following the [Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor") on December 7, 1941, the United States entered [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), helping defeat the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers"); [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy "Fascist Italy") in the [European theater](/wiki/European_theatre_of_World_War_II "European theatre of World War II") and, in the [Pacific War](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War"), [Imperial Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") after using [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon") on [Hiroshima and Nagasaki](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki") in August 1945. The war led to the [US led Allied occupation of Japan](/wiki/Occupation_of_Japan "Occupation of Japan") until 1952 and the [Allied-occupied Germany](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Germany "Allied-occupied Germany").

Following the end of World War II, the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") commenced with the United States and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") emerging as [superpower](/wiki/Superpower "Superpower") rivals; the two countries largely confronted each other indirectly in the [arms race](/wiki/Arms_race "Arms race"), the [Space Race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race"), propaganda campaigns, and [proxy wars](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war"), which included the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") and the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"). In the 1960s, due largely to the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"), social reforms enforced African Americans' constitutional rights of voting and freedom of movement. In 1991, the United States [led a coalition](/wiki/Coalition_of_the_Gulf_War "Coalition of the Gulf War") and invaded [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") during the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"). Later in the year, the Cold War ended with the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), leaving the United States as the world's sole superpower.

In the [post-Cold War era](/wiki/Post%E2%80%93Cold_War_era "Post–Cold War era"), the United States has been drawn into [conflicts in the Middle East](/wiki/List_of_modern_conflicts_in_the_Middle_East "List of modern conflicts in the Middle East"), especially following the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), with the start of the [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror"). In the 21st century, the country was negatively impacted by the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession_in_the_United_States "Great Recession in the United States") of 2007 to 2009 and the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") of 2020 to 2023. Recently, the U.S. [withdrew from the war in Afghanistan](/wiki/2020%E2%80%932021_U.S._troop_withdrawal_from_Afghanistan "2020–2021 U.S. troop withdrawal from Afghanistan"), [intervened in the Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/United_States_and_the_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "United States and the Russian invasion of Ukraine"), and became militarily involved in the [Middle Eastern crisis](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_crisis_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Middle Eastern crisis (2023–present)"), which included the [Red Sea crisis](/wiki/Red_Sea_crisis "Red Sea crisis"), a military conflict between the U.S., and the [Houthi movement](/wiki/Houthi_movement "Houthi movement") in [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen"), and both the [2025](/wiki/United_States_strikes_on_Iranian_nuclear_sites "United States strikes on Iranian nuclear sites") and [2026](/wiki/2026_Israeli%E2%80%93United_States_strikes_on_Iran "2026 Israeli–United States strikes on Iran") strikes in Iran during the [Iran–Israel war](/wiki/Twelve-Day_War "Twelve-Day War").

## Indigenous inhabitants

Main articles: [Geological history of North America](/wiki/Geological_history_of_North_America "Geological history of North America"), [History of Native Americans in the United States](/wiki/History_of_Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "History of Native Americans in the United States"), and [Pre-Columbian era](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era")

See also: [Native Americans in the United States](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Peopling_of_America_through_Beringia.png/250px-Peopling_of_America_through_Beringia.png)](/wiki/File:Peopling_of_America_through_Beringia.png)

Approximate location of the ice-free corridor and specific Paleoindian sites, according to the [Clovis theory](/wiki/Clovis_theory "Clovis theory")

It is not definitively known how or when Native Americans first [settled the Americas](/wiki/Settlement_of_the_Americas "Settlement of the Americas"). The prevailing theory proposes that people from [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia") followed [game](/wiki/Game_(hunting) "Game (hunting)") across [Beringia](/wiki/Beringia "Beringia"), a [land bridge](/wiki/Land_bridge "Land bridge") that connected [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") to present-day [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") during the [Ice Age](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period"), and then spread southward, perhaps as early as 30,000 years ago.[[5]](#cite_note-5) These early inhabitants, called [Paleo-Indians](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians"), soon diversified into hundreds of culturally distinct groups.

### Paleo-Indians

Main article: [Pre-Columbian North America](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_North_America "Pre-Columbian North America")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/North_American_cultural_areas.png/250px-North_American_cultural_areas.png)](/wiki/File:North_American_cultural_areas.png)

The [cultural areas](/wiki/Cultural_area "Cultural area") of [pre-Columbian North America](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_North_America "Pre-Columbian North America"), according to [Alfred Kroeber](/wiki/Alfred_Kroeber "Alfred Kroeber")

By 10,000 BCE, humans had already been well-established throughout North America. Originally, Paleo-Indians hunted Ice Age [megafauna](/wiki/Megafauna "Megafauna") like [mammoths](/wiki/Mammoth "Mammoth"), but as they began to go extinct, people turned instead to [bison](/wiki/Bison "Bison") as a food source, and later foraging for berries and seeds. Paleo-Indians in central Mexico were the first in the Americas to farm, around 8,000 BCE. Eventually, the knowledge began to spread northward. By 3,000 BCE, corn was being grown in the valleys of [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") and [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), followed by primitive [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation") systems and, by 300 BCE, early villages of the [Hohokam](/wiki/Hohokam "Hohokam").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutline_of_American_History-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChenault-7)

One of the earlier cultures in the present-day United States was the [Clovis culture](/wiki/Clovis_culture "Clovis culture") (9,100 to 8,850 BCE), who are primarily identified by the use of fluted [spear](/wiki/Spear "Spear") points called the [Clovis point](/wiki/Clovis_point "Clovis point"). The [Folsom culture](/wiki/Folsom_culture "Folsom culture") was similar, but is marked by the use of the [Folsom point](/wiki/Folsom_point "Folsom point").

A later migration around 8,000 BCE included [Na-Dene](/wiki/Na-Dene "Na-Dene")-speaking peoples, who reached the [Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest") by 5,000 BCE.[[8]](#cite_note-8) From there, they migrated along the [Pacific Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") and into the interior.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Another group, the [Oshara tradition](/wiki/Oshara_tradition "Oshara tradition") people, who lived from 5,500 BCE to 600 CE, were part of the [Archaic Southwest](/wiki/Archaic_Southwest "Archaic Southwest").

### Mound builders and pueblos

Main articles: [Mound Builders](/wiki/Mound_Builders "Mound Builders") and [Ancestral Puebloans](/wiki/Ancestral_Puebloans "Ancestral Puebloans")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Monks_Mound_in_July.JPG/250px-Monks_Mound_in_July.JPG)](/wiki/File:Monks_Mound_in_July.JPG)

[Monks Mound](/wiki/Monks_Mound "Monks Mound") of [Cahokia](/wiki/Cahokia "Cahokia"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site"), in summer

The [Adena](/wiki/Adena_culture "Adena culture") began constructing large [earthwork](/wiki/Earthworks_(archaeology) "Earthworks (archaeology)") mounds around 600 BCE. They are the earliest known people to have been [Mound Builders](/wiki/Mound_Builders "Mound Builders"), although there are [mounds](/wiki/Mound "Mound") in the United States that predate this culture. The Adenans were absorbed into the [Hopewell tradition](/wiki/Hopewell_tradition "Hopewell tradition"), a powerful people who traded tools and goods across a wide territory. They continued the Adena tradition of mound-building and pioneered a trading system called the Hopewell Exchange System, which at its greatest extent ran from the present-day Southeast up to the Canadian side of [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario").[[10]](#cite_note-OHC-2011-10) By 500 CE, the Hopewellians had been absorbed into the larger [Mississippian culture](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture").

The Mississippians were a broad group of tribes. Their most important city was [Cahokia](/wiki/Cahokia "Cahokia"), near modern-day [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"). At its peak in the 12th century, the city had an estimated population of 20,000, larger than the population of London at the time. The entire city was centered around the [Monks Mound](/wiki/Monks_Mound "Monks Mound") that stood 100 feet (30 m) tall. Cahokia, like many other cities and villages of the time, depended on hunting, foraging, trading, and agriculture, and developed a class system with slaves and human sacrifice that was influenced by societies to the south, like the [Mayans](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutline_of_American_History-6)

In the [Southwest](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States"), the [Anasazi](/wiki/Anasazi "Anasazi") began constructing stone and adobe pueblos around 900 BCE.[[11]](#cite_note-eb-11) These apartment-like structures were often built into cliff faces, as seen in the [Cliff Palace](/wiki/Cliff_Palace "Cliff Palace") at [Mesa Verde](/wiki/Mesa_Verde "Mesa Verde"). Some grew to be the size of cities, with [Pueblo Bonito](/wiki/Pueblo_Bonito "Pueblo Bonito") along the [Chaco River](/wiki/Chaco_River "Chaco River") in New Mexico once consisting of 800 rooms.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutline_of_American_History-6)

### Northwest and northeast

Main articles: [Indigenous peoples of the Eastern Woodlands](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Eastern_Woodlands "Indigenous peoples of the Eastern Woodlands") and [Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Pacific_Northwest_Coast "Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Tlingit_K%27alyaan_Totem_Pole_August_2005.jpg/250px-Tlingit_K%27alyaan_Totem_Pole_August_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tlingit_K%27alyaan_Totem_Pole_August_2005.jpg)

The *K'alyaan* [Totem pole](/wiki/Totem_pole "Totem pole") of the [Tlingit](/wiki/Tlingit "Tlingit") Kiks.ádi Clan, erected at [Sitka National Historical Park](/wiki/Sitka_National_Historical_Park "Sitka National Historical Park") to commemorate the lives lost in the [Battle of Sitka](/wiki/Battle_of_Sitka "Battle of Sitka") in 1804

The [Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Pacific_Northwest "Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest") were likely the most affluent Native Americans. Many distinct cultural groups and political entities developed there, but they all shared certain beliefs, traditions, and practices, such as the centrality of [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon") as a resource and spiritual symbol. Permanent villages began to develop in this region as early as 1,000 BCE, and these communities celebrated by the gift-giving feast of the [potlatch](/wiki/Potlatch "Potlatch").

In present-day [upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"), the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") formed a [confederacy](/wiki/Confederacy "Confederacy") of tribal peoples in the mid-15th century, consisting of the [Oneida](/wiki/Oneida_Indian_Nation "Oneida Indian Nation"), [Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawk_people "Mohawk people"), [Onondaga](/wiki/Onondaga_people "Onondaga people"), [Cayuga](/wiki/Cayuga_people "Cayuga people"), and [Seneca](/wiki/Seneca_people "Seneca people").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETooker107–128-14) Each tribe was represented in a group of 50 [sachem chiefs](/wiki/Sachem "Sachem"). It has been suggested that their culture contributed to political thinking during the development of the United States government.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Iroquois were powerful, waging war with many neighboring tribes, and later, Europeans. As their territory expanded, smaller tribes were forced further west, including the [Osage](/wiki/Osage_Nation "Osage Nation"), [Kaw](/wiki/Kaw_people "Kaw people"), [Ponca](/wiki/Ponca "Ponca"), and [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha_people "Omaha people") peoples.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETooker107–128-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Burns-15)

### Native Hawaiians

Main articles: [Ancient Hawaii](/wiki/Ancient_Hawaii "Ancient Hawaii") and [Hawaiian Kingdom](/wiki/Hawaiian_Kingdom "Hawaiian Kingdom")

The exact date for the settling of Hawaii is disputed but the first settlement most likely took place between 940 and 1130 CE.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Around 1200 CE, [Tahitian](/wiki/Tahitians "Tahitians") explorers found and began settling the area. This marked the rise of the Hawaiian civilization, which would be largely separated from the rest of the world until the arrival of the British 600 years later.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Europeans under the British explorer [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") arrived in the Hawaiian Islands in 1778, and within five years of contact, European military technology would help [Kamehameha I](/wiki/Kamehameha_I "Kamehameha I") conquer most of the island group, and eventually unify the islands for the first time, establishing the [Hawaiian Kingdom](/wiki/Hawaiian_Kingdom "Hawaiian Kingdom").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Puerto Rico

Main articles: [History of Puerto Rico](/wiki/History_of_Puerto_Rico "History of Puerto Rico") and [Taino](/wiki/Taino "Taino")

The island of Puerto Rico has been settled for at least 4,000 years. Starting with the [Ortoiroid culture](/wiki/Ortoiroid_culture "Ortoiroid culture"), successive generations of native migrations arrived replacing or absorbing local populations. By the year 1000 [Arawak](/wiki/Arawak "Arawak") people had arrived from South America via the [Lesser Antilles](/wiki/Lesser_Antilles "Lesser Antilles"); these settlers would become the [Taíno](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") encountered by the Spanish in 1493. Upon European contact a native population between 30,000 and 60,000 was likely, led by a single chief called a [Cacique](/wiki/Cacique "Cacique").[[21]](#cite_note-Rouse_1992-21) Colonization resulted in the decimation of the local inhabitants due to the harsh [Encomienda](/wiki/Encomienda "Encomienda") system and epidemics caused by Old World diseases. Puerto Rico would remain a part of Spain until American annexation in 1898.[[21]](#cite_note-Rouse_1992-21)

## European colonization (1075–1754)

Main article: [Colonial history of the United States](/wiki/Colonial_history_of_the_United_States "Colonial history of the United States")

### Norse exploration

Main article: [Norse colonization of North America](/wiki/Norse_colonization_of_North_America "Norse colonization of North America")

The earliest recorded European mention of America is in a [treatise](/wiki/Gesta_Hammaburgensis_ecclesiae_pontificum "Gesta Hammaburgensis ecclesiae pontificum") by the medieval chronicler [Adam of Bremen](/wiki/Adam_of_Bremen "Adam of Bremen"), circa 1075, where it is referred to as [Vinland](/wiki/Vinland "Vinland").[[a]](#cite_note-23) It is also extensively referred to in the Norse [Vinland sagas](/wiki/Vinland_sagas "Vinland sagas"). The strongest archaeological evidence of the existence of [Norse](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen") settlements in America is located in Canada; there is significant scholarly debate as to whether Norse explorers also made landfall in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England").[[23]](#cite_note-24)

### Early settlements

Main article: [European colonization of the Americas](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Mayflower_in_Plymouth_Harbor%2C_by_William_Halsall.jpg/250px-Mayflower_in_Plymouth_Harbor%2C_by_William_Halsall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mayflower_in_Plymouth_Harbor,_by_William_Halsall.jpg)

The *[Mayflower](/wiki/Mayflower "Mayflower")* in [Plymouth Harbor](/wiki/Plymouth_Harbor "Plymouth Harbor"). [Fluyts](/wiki/Fluyt "Fluyt"), [caravels](/wiki/Caravel "Caravel"), and [carracks](/wiki/Carrack "Carrack") brought Europeans to the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").

Europeans brought horses, cattle, and hogs to the Americas and took back maize, turkeys, tomatoes, potatoes, tobacco, beans, and [squash](/wiki/Cucurbita "Cucurbita") to Europe. Many explorers and early settlers died after being exposed to new diseases in the Americas. However, the effects of new Eurasian diseases carried by the colonists, especially smallpox and measles, were much worse for the Native Americans, as they had no [immunity](/wiki/Immunity_(medical) "Immunity (medical)") to them. They [suffered epidemics](/wiki/Native_American_disease_and_epidemics "Native American disease and epidemics") and died in very large numbers, usually before large-scale European settlement began. Their societies were disrupted by the scale of deaths.[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26)

#### Spanish contact

Main articles: [Spanish Florida](/wiki/Spanish_Florida "Spanish Florida"), [Santa Fe de Nuevo México](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México"), and [Spanish Texas](/wiki/Spanish_Texas "Spanish Texas")

See also: [Spanish colonization of the Americas](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas") and [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain")

[Spanish explorers](/wiki/Conquistador "Conquistador") were the first Europeans, after the Norse, to reach the present-day United States, after the [voyages of Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Voyages_of_Christopher_Columbus "Voyages of Christopher Columbus") (beginning in 1492) established [possessions in the Caribbean](/wiki/Spanish_West_Indies "Spanish West Indies"), including the modern-day [U.S. territories](/wiki/U.S._territories "U.S. territories") of [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Puerto_Rico "Captaincy General of Puerto Rico"), and parts of the [U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/U.S._Virgin_Islands "U.S. Virgin Islands"). [Juan Ponce de León](/wiki/Juan_Ponce_de_Le%C3%B3n "Juan Ponce de León") landed in [Florida](/wiki/Spanish_Florida "Spanish Florida") in 1513.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Spanish expeditions quickly reached the [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"), the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon"),[[27]](#cite_note-28) and the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDay-29)

In 1539, [Hernando de Soto](/wiki/Hernando_de_Soto "Hernando de Soto") extensively explored the Southeast,[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDay-29) and a year later [Francisco Coronado](/wiki/Francisco_Coronado "Francisco Coronado") explored from Arizona to central Kansas in search of gold.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDay-29) Escaped horses from Coronado's party spread over the Great Plains, and the Plains Indians mastered horsemanship within a few generations.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutline_of_American_History-6) Small Spanish settlements eventually grew to become important cities, such as [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"), [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque "Albuquerque"), [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson "Tucson"), Los Angeles, and San Francisco.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

#### Dutch mid-Atlantic

Main article: [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland")

See also: [Dutch colonization of the Americas](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas")

The [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") sent explorer [Henry Hudson](/wiki/Henry_Hudson "Henry Hudson") to search for a [Northwest Passage](/wiki/Northwest_Passage "Northwest Passage") to Asia in 1609. [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") was established in 1621 by the company to capitalize on the [North American fur trade](/wiki/North_American_fur_trade "North American fur trade"). Growth was slow at first due to mismanagement by the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas") and Native American conflicts. After the Dutch purchased the island of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") from the Native Americans, the land was named [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") and became the capital of New Netherland. The town rapidly expanded and in the mid-1600s it became an important trading center. Despite being [Calvinists](/wiki/Calvinists "Calvinists") and building the [Reformed Church in America](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America"), the Dutch were tolerant of other religions and cultures and traded with the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") to the north.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-31)

The colony served as a barrier to British expansion from [New England](/wiki/New_England_Confederation "New England Confederation"), and as a result a [series of wars](/wiki/Anglo-Dutch_Wars "Anglo-Dutch Wars") were fought. The colony was taken over by Britain as [New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York") in 1664 and its capital was renamed New York City.

#### Swedish settlement

Main article: [New Sweden](/wiki/New_Sweden "New Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Nothnagle_Log_House.JPG/250px-Nothnagle_Log_House.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nothnagle_Log_House.JPG)

[C. A. Nothnagle Log House](/wiki/C._A._Nothnagle_Log_House "C. A. Nothnagle Log House") in [Gibbstown, New Jersey](/wiki/Gibbstown,_New_Jersey "Gibbstown, New Jersey"), the oldest wooden building in the United States

In the early years of the [Swedish Empire](/wiki/Sweden#Swedish_Empire "Sweden"), Swedish, Dutch, and German stockholders formed the [New Sweden Company](/wiki/New_Sweden_Company "New Sweden Company") to trade furs and tobacco in North America. The company's first expedition was led by [Peter Minuit](/wiki/Peter_Minuit "Peter Minuit"), who had been governor of New Netherland from 1626 to 1631, and landed in [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay") in March 1638. The settlers founded [Fort Christina](/wiki/Fort_Christina "Fort Christina") at the site of modern-day [Wilmington, Delaware](/wiki/Wilmington,_Delaware "Wilmington, Delaware"), and made treaties with Indigenous peoples for land ownership on both sides of the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River").[[31]](#cite_note-SCS-2021-32)[[32]](#cite_note-genealogia.fi-2020-33)

Over the following seventeen years, 12 more expeditions brought settlers from the Swedish Empire to New Sweden. The colony established 19 permanent settlements along with many farms, extending into modern-day [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), and [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). It was incorporated into New Netherland in 1655 after a Dutch invasion from the neighboring New Netherland colony during the [Second Northern War](/wiki/Second_Northern_War "Second Northern War").[[31]](#cite_note-SCS-2021-32)[[32]](#cite_note-genealogia.fi-2020-33)

#### French

Main articles: [French Florida](/wiki/French_Florida "French Florida"), [Canada (New France)](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)"), [Louisiana (New France)](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)"), [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country"), and [Ohio Country](/wiki/Ohio_Country "Ohio Country")

See also: [French colonization of the Americas](/wiki/French_colonization_of_the_Americas "French colonization of the Americas") and [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/San_Pablo_Bastion%2C_Castillo_de_San_Marcos.jpg/250px-San_Pablo_Bastion%2C_Castillo_de_San_Marcos.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Pablo_Bastion,_Castillo_de_San_Marcos.jpg)

The San Pablo Bastion of the [Castillo de San Marcos](/wiki/Castillo_de_San_Marcos "Castillo de San Marcos"), completed in 1683, in [St. Augustine, Florida](/wiki/History_of_St._Augustine,_Florida "History of St. Augustine, Florida")

[Giovanni da Verrazzano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Verrazzano "Giovanni da Verrazzano") landed in [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina") in 1524, and was the first European to sail into [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") and [Narragansett Bay](/wiki/Narragansett_Bay "Narragansett Bay"). In the 1540s, French [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots") settled at [Fort Caroline](/wiki/Fort_Caroline "Fort Caroline") near present-day [Jacksonville, Florida](/wiki/Jacksonville,_Florida "Jacksonville, Florida"). In 1565, Spanish forces led by [Pedro Menéndez](/wiki/Pedro_Men%C3%A9ndez "Pedro Menéndez") destroyed the settlement and established the first Spanish settlement in what would become the United States — [St. Augustine](/wiki/St._Augustine,_Florida "St. Augustine, Florida").

Most French lived in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") and [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") (modern Canada), but far-reaching trade relationships with Native Americans spread their influence. French colonists in small villages along the Mississippi and [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River") rivers lived in farming communities that served as a grain source for Gulf Coast settlements. The French established plantations in Louisiana along with settling [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), [Mobile](/wiki/Mobile,_Alabama "Mobile, Alabama"), and [Biloxi](/wiki/Biloxi "Biloxi").

### British colonies

Further information: [British colonization of the Americas](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas") and [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America")

The English, drawn in by [Francis Drake](/wiki/Francis_Drake "Francis Drake")'s raids on [Spanish treasure ships](/wiki/Spanish_treasure_fleet "Spanish treasure fleet") leaving the New World, settled the strip of land along the east coast in the 1600s.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutline_of_American_History-6) The early British colonies were established by private groups seeking profit, and were marked by starvation, disease, and Native American attacks. Many immigrants were people seeking religious freedom or escaping political oppression, peasants displaced by the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), or those simply seeking adventure and opportunity. Between the late 1610s and the Revolution, the British shipped an estimated 50,000 to 120,000 convicts to their American colonies.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

In some areas, Native Americans taught colonists how to grow local crops. In others, they attacked the settlers. Virgin forests provided an ample supply of building material and firewood. Natural inlets and harbors lined the coast, providing easy ports for essential trade with Europe. Settlements remained close to the coast due to this as well as Native American resistance and the Appalachian Mountains in the interior.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutline_of_American_History-6)

#### First settlement in Jamestown

Main articles: [Jamestown, Virginia](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia") and [Colony of Virginia](/wiki/Colony_of_Virginia "Colony of Virginia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Matth%C3%A4us_Merian_d%C3%86%2C_S%C3%A5ledes_blev_ogs%C3%A5_denne_fred_brudt_p%C3%A5_grund_af_bedrag%2C_KKSgb10894-5%2C_Statens_Museum_for_Kunst.jpg/250px-Matth%C3%A4us_Merian_d%C3%86%2C_S%C3%A5ledes_blev_ogs%C3%A5_denne_fred_brudt_p%C3%A5_grund_af_bedrag%2C_KKSgb10894-5%2C_Statens_Museum_for_Kunst.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matth%C3%A4us_Merian_d%C3%86,_S%C3%A5ledes_blev_ogs%C3%A5_denne_fred_brudt_p%C3%A5_grund_af_bedrag,_KKSgb10894-5,_Statens_Museum_for_Kunst.jpg)

Following the [Indian massacre of Jamestown settlers](/wiki/Indian_massacre_of_1622 "Indian massacre of 1622") in 1622, colonists in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") feared all natives as enemies.

The first successful English colony, [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia"), was established by the [Virginia Company](/wiki/Virginia_Company_of_London "Virginia Company of London") in 1607 on the [James River](/wiki/James_River "James River") in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"). The colonists were preoccupied with the search for gold and were ill-equipped for life in the New World. Captain [John Smith](/wiki/John_Smith_(explorer) "John Smith (explorer)") held the fledgling Jamestown together in the first year, and the colony descended into anarchy and nearly failed when he returned to England two years later. [John Rolfe](/wiki/John_Rolfe "John Rolfe") began experimenting with tobacco from the West Indies in 1612, and by 1614 the first shipment arrived in London. It became Virginia's chief source of revenue within a decade.

In 1624, after years of disease and Indian attacks, including the [Powhatan attack of 1622](/wiki/Powhatan_attack_of_1622 "Powhatan attack of 1622"), King [James I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") revoked the Virginia Company's charter and made Virginia a royal colony.

#### New England Colonies

Main articles: [New England Colonies](/wiki/New_England_Colonies "New England Colonies") and [Puritan migration to New England (1620–1640)](/wiki/Puritan_migration_to_New_England_(1620%E2%80%931640) "Puritan migration to New England (1620–1640)")

[New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") was initially settled primarily by [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") fleeing religious persecution. The [Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrims_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrims (Plymouth Colony)") sailed for Virginia on the Mayflower in 1620, but were knocked off course by a storm and landed at [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony"), where they agreed to a social contract of rules in the [Mayflower Compact](/wiki/Mayflower_Compact "Mayflower Compact"). About half died in the first winter.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Like Jamestown, Plymouth suffered from disease and starvation, but local [Wampanoag](/wiki/Wampanoag "Wampanoag") Indians taught the colonists how to farm maize.

Plymouth was followed by the Puritans and [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") in 1630. They maintained a charter for self-government separate from England, and elected founder [John Winthrop](/wiki/John_Winthrop "John Winthrop") as governor. [Roger Williams](/wiki/Roger_Williams "Roger Williams") opposed Winthrop's treatment of Native Americans and religious intolerance, and established the colony of [Providence Plantations](/wiki/Providence_Plantations "Providence Plantations"), later [Rhode Island](/wiki/Colony_of_Rhode_Island_and_Providence_Plantations "Colony of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations"), on the basis of freedom of religion. Other colonists established settlements in the [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River") Valley, and on the coasts of present-day [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") and [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"). Native American attacks continued, with the most significant occurring in the 1637 [Pequot War](/wiki/Pequot_War "Pequot War") and the 1675 [King Philip's War](/wiki/King_Philip%27s_War "King Philip's War").

New England became a center of commerce and industry due to the poor, mountainous soil making agriculture difficult. Rivers were harnessed to power grain mills and sawmills, and the numerous harbors facilitated trade. Tight-knit villages developed around these industrial centers, and [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") became one of America's most important ports.

#### Middle Colonies

Main article: [Middle Colonies](/wiki/Middle_Colonies "Middle Colonies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Treaty_of_Penn_with_Indians_by_Benjamin_West.jpg/250px-Treaty_of_Penn_with_Indians_by_Benjamin_West.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treaty_of_Penn_with_Indians_by_Benjamin_West.jpg)

*Treaty of Penn with the Indians*, a portrait depicting [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn") signing the [Treaty of Shackamaxon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Shackamaxon "Treaty of Shackamaxon") with [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") Indians in the [Province of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Province_of_Pennsylvania "Province of Pennsylvania") in 1682 by [Benjamin West](/wiki/Benjamin_West "Benjamin West")

In the 1660s, the [Middle Colonies](/wiki/Middle_Colonies "Middle Colonies") of [New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York"), [New Jersey](/wiki/Province_of_New_Jersey "Province of New Jersey"), and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_Colony "Delaware Colony") were established in the former Dutch New Netherland, and were characterized by a large degree of ethnic and religious diversity. At the same time, the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") of New York, strengthened by years of fur trading with Europeans, formed the powerful Iroquois Confederacy.

The last colony in this region was [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Province_of_Pennsylvania "Province of Pennsylvania"), established in 1681 by [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn") as a home for religious dissenters, including [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), [Methodists](/wiki/Methodists "Methodists"), and the [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiddleton_and_Lombard-36) The capital of the colony, [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), became a dominant commercial center in a few short years. While Quakers populated the city, [German](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") immigrants began to flood into the Pennsylvanian hills and forests, while the [Scots-Irish](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_Americans "Scotch-Irish Americans") pushed into the far western frontier.

#### Southern Colonies

Main article: [Southern Colonies](/wiki/Southern_Colonies "Southern Colonies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Gullah_s_carolina_1790.jpg/250px-Gullah_s_carolina_1790.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gullah_s_carolina_1790.jpg)

*The Old Plantation*, a portrait depicting a plantation in [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina") in approximately 1790 with [Gullah](/wiki/Gullah "Gullah") slaves playing traditional [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa") instruments resisting forced assimilation from the planation culture.

The overwhelmingly rural [Southern Colonies](/wiki/Southern_Colonies "Southern Colonies") contrasted sharply with the New England and Middle Colonies. After Virginia, the second British colony south of New England was [Maryland](/wiki/Province_of_Maryland "Province of Maryland"), established as a Catholic haven in 1632. The economy of these two colonies was built entirely on [yeoman](/wiki/Yeoman "Yeoman") farmers and planters. The planters established themselves in the [Tidewater](/wiki/Tidewater_(region) "Tidewater (region)") region of Virginia, establishing massive [plantations](/wiki/Plantation_complexes_in_the_Southern_United_States "Plantation complexes in the Southern United States") with slave labor.

In 1670, the [Province of Carolina](/wiki/Province_of_Carolina "Province of Carolina") was established, and [Charleston](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina") became the region's great trading port. While Virginia's economy was also based on tobacco, Carolina was more diversified, exporting rice, indigo, and lumber as well. In 1712, it was divided in two, creating [North](/wiki/Province_of_North_Carolina "Province of North Carolina") and [South Carolina](/wiki/Province_of_South_Carolina "Province of South Carolina"). The [Georgia Colony](/wiki/Georgia_Colony "Georgia Colony") was established by [James Oglethorpe](/wiki/James_Oglethorpe "James Oglethorpe") in 1732 as a border to [Spanish Florida](/wiki/Spanish_Florida "Spanish Florida") and a reform colony for former prisoners and the poor.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiddleton_and_Lombard-36)

#### Religion

Main article: [History of religion in the United States](/wiki/History_of_religion_in_the_United_States "History of religion in the United States")

Religiosity expanded greatly after the [First Great Awakening](/wiki/First_Great_Awakening "First Great Awakening"), a religious revival in the 1740s led by preachers such as [Jonathan Edwards](/wiki/Jonathan_Edwards_(theologian) "Jonathan Edwards (theologian)") and [George Whitefield](/wiki/George_Whitefield "George Whitefield"). American [Evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicals "Evangelicals") affected by the Awakening added a new emphasis on divine outpourings of the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") and conversions that implanted new believers with an intense love for God. Revivals encapsulated those hallmarks and carried the newly created evangelicalism into the early republic, setting the stage for the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") in the late 1790s.[[36]](#cite_note-37) In the early stages, evangelicals in the South, such as [Methodists](/wiki/Methodists "Methodists") and [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists_in_the_United_States "Baptists in the United States"), preached for religious freedom and abolition of slavery.

#### Government

Main article: [Colonial government in the Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Colonial_government_in_the_Thirteen_Colonies "Colonial government in the Thirteen Colonies")

Each of the American colonies had a slightly different governmental structure. Typically, a colony was ruled by a governor appointed from London who controlled the executive administration and relied upon a locally elected legislature to vote on taxes and make laws. By the 18th century, the American colonies were growing very rapidly as a result of low death rates along with ample supplies of land and food. The colonies were richer than most parts of Britain, and attracted a steady flow of immigrants, especially teenagers who arrived as indentured servants.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESavelle185–190-38)

#### Servitude and slavery

Main articles: [Indentured servitude in British America](/wiki/Indentured_servitude_in_British_America "Indentured servitude in British America") and [Slavery in the colonial history of the United States](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_colonial_history_of_the_United_States "Slavery in the colonial history of the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Nouvelle-France_map-en.svg/250px-Nouvelle-France_map-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nouvelle-France_map-en.svg)

A map of the [British](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas"), [French](/wiki/New_France "New France") and [Spanish](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") settlements in North America in 1750, before the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War")

Over half of all European immigrants to Colonial America arrived as [indentured servants](/wiki/Indentured_servants "Indentured servants").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Typically, people would sign a contract agreeing to a set term of labor, usually four to seven years, and in return would receive transport to America and a piece of land at the end of their servitude. In some cases, ships' captains received rewards for the delivery of poor migrants, and so extravagant promises and kidnapping were common.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutline_of_American_History-6)

The first [African slaves](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States") arrived in 1619.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECorbett_et_al.-39) Initially regarded as indentured servants who could buy their freedom, the institution of slavery began to harden and the involuntary servitude became lifelong[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECorbett_et_al.-39) as the demand for labor on tobacco and rice plantations grew in the 1660s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Slavery became identified with brown skin color, and the children of slave women were born slaves, known as *[partus sequitur ventrem](/wiki/Partus_sequitur_ventrem "Partus sequitur ventrem")*.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECorbett_et_al.-39) By the 1770s, African slaves comprised a fifth of the American population.

The question of independence from Britain did not arise as long as the colonies needed British military support against the French and Spanish powers. Those threats were gone by 1765. However, London continued to regard the American colonies as existing for the benefit of the mother country in a policy known as [mercantilism](/wiki/Mercantilism "Mercantilism").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESavelle185–190-38)

Colonial America was defined by a severe labor shortage that used forms of [unfree labor](/wiki/Unfree_labor "Unfree labor"), such as [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_colonial_history_of_the_United_States "Slavery in the colonial history of the United States") and indentured servitude. The British colonies were also marked by a policy of avoiding strict enforcement of parliamentary laws, known as [salutary neglect](/wiki/Salutary_neglect "Salutary neglect"). This permitted the development of an American spirit distinct from that of its European founders.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEncarta_Online-40)

## Revolutionary period (1754–1793)

Main article: [History of the United States (1776–1789)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1776%E2%80%931789) "History of the United States (1776–1789)")

### Lead-up to the Revolution

Further information: [Boston Tea Party](/wiki/Boston_Tea_Party "Boston Tea Party")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Boston_Tea_Party_Currier_colored.jpg/250px-Boston_Tea_Party_Currier_colored.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boston_Tea_Party_Currier_colored.jpg)

A portrait depicting the [Boston Tea Party](/wiki/Boston_Tea_Party "Boston Tea Party") on December 16, 1773, a prominent act of rebellion that served to dramatically escalate the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), leading ultimately to the commencement of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") at the [Battles of Lexington and Concord](/wiki/Battles_of_Lexington_and_Concord "Battles of Lexington and Concord") on April 19, 1775

The [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") (1754–1763), part of the larger [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), was a watershed event in the political development of the colonies. The influence of the French and Native Americans, the main rivals of the [British Crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown") in the colonies and Canada, was significantly reduced and the territory of the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") expanded into [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") in Canada and [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The war effort also resulted in greater political integration of the colonies, as reflected in the [Albany Congress](/wiki/Albany_Congress "Albany Congress") and symbolized by [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin")'s call for the colonies to "[Join, or Die](/wiki/Join,_or_Die "Join, or Die")."[[40]](#cite_note-41)

[King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III") issued the [Royal Proclamation of 1763](/wiki/Royal_Proclamation_of_1763 "Royal Proclamation of 1763"), to organize the new North American empire and protect the Native Americans from colonial expansion west of the Appalachian Mountains. Strains developed in the relations between the colonists and the Crown. The [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament") passed the [Stamp Act of 1765](/wiki/Stamp_Act_of_1765 "Stamp Act of 1765"), imposing a tax on the colonies without going through the colonial legislatures. Crying "[No taxation without representation](/wiki/No_taxation_without_representation "No taxation without representation")", the colonists refused to pay.[[41]](#cite_note-42) On March 5, 1770, in an event that would fuel anti-British sentiment throughout the colonies, a violent confrontation between a local mob and British troops in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") led to the deaths of five Bostonians in what became known as the [Boston Massacre](/wiki/Boston_Massacre "Boston Massacre").

On December 16, 1773, the [Boston Tea Party](/wiki/Boston_Tea_Party "Boston Tea Party") was a direct action to protest the new tax on tea. Parliament responded the next year with the [Intolerable Acts](/wiki/Intolerable_Acts "Intolerable Acts"), stripping Massachusetts of its historic right of self-government and putting it under military rule, which sparked outrage and resistance in all thirteen colonies. [Patriot](/wiki/Patriot_(American_Revolution) "Patriot (American Revolution)") leaders from every colony convened the [First Continental Congress](/wiki/First_Continental_Congress "First Continental Congress") to coordinate their resistance. The Congress called for a [boycott of British trade](/wiki/Continental_Association "Continental Association"), published a [list of rights and grievances](/wiki/Declaration_and_Resolves_of_the_First_Continental_Congress "Declaration and Resolves of the First Continental Congress"), and [petitioned the king](/wiki/Petition_to_the_King "Petition to the King") to rectify those grievances.[[42]](#cite_note-43) This appeal had no effect.

### American Revolution

Main article: [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Official_Presidential_portrait_of_Thomas_Jefferson_%28by_Rembrandt_Peale%2C_1800%29%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Official_Presidential_portrait_of_Thomas_Jefferson_%28by_Rembrandt_Peale%2C_1800%29%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_Presidential_portrait_of_Thomas_Jefferson_(by_Rembrandt_Peale,_1800)(cropped).jpg)

[Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson"), the principal author of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") and a principle intellectual force behind the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), wrote the first draft of the Declaration in isolation over a period of two weeks between June 11, 1776, and June 28, 1776, from the second floor of a three-story home he was renting at 700 [Market Street](/wiki/Market_Street_(Philadelphia) "Market Street (Philadelphia)") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). The Declaration was unanimously adopted by the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") a week later, on July 4, 1776, at present-day [Independence Hall](/wiki/Independence_Hall "Independence Hall")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_by_Emanuel_Leutze%2C_MMA-NYC%2C_1851.jpg/250px-Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_by_Emanuel_Leutze%2C_MMA-NYC%2C_1851.jpg)](/wiki/File:Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_by_Emanuel_Leutze,_MMA-NYC,_1851.jpg)

[Washington's covert crossing of the Delaware River](/wiki/George_Washington%27s_crossing_of_the_Delaware_River "George Washington's crossing of the Delaware River") over the night of December 25–26, 1776, represented a major comeback for the cause of American independence following the loss of New York City, allowing Washington and the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") to launch surprise attacks on [British](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_American_Revolutionary_War "British Army during the American Revolutionary War") and [Hessian](/wiki/Hessian_(soldier) "Hessian (soldier)") troops in [Trenton](/wiki/Battle_of_Trenton "Battle of Trenton") and [Princeton](/wiki/Battle_of_Princeton "Battle of Princeton") and recapture [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").

The [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") voted to declare independence on July 2, 1776. The [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") presented arguments in favor of the rights of citizens, stating that [all men are created equal](/wiki/All_men_are_created_equal "All men are created equal"), supporting the rights of [Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Happiness](/wiki/Life,_Liberty_and_the_pursuit_of_Happiness "Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Happiness"), and demanding the [consent of the governed](/wiki/Consent_of_the_governed "Consent of the governed").[[43]](#cite_note-Hamowy-2000-44) The [Founding Fathers](/wiki/Founding_Fathers "Founding Fathers") were guided by the ideology of [republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism "Republicanism"), rejecting the [monarchism](/wiki/Monarchism "Monarchism") then dominant in Europe.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The Declaration of Independence was [signed](/wiki/Signing_of_the_United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "Signing of the United States Declaration of Independence") by members of the Congress on July 4.[[43]](#cite_note-Hamowy-2000-44) This date has since been [commemorated](/wiki/Commemoration_of_the_American_Revolution "Commemoration of the American Revolution") as [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(United_States) "Independence Day (United States)").[[45]](#cite_note-46)

The [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") began with the [Battles of Lexington and Concord](/wiki/Battles_of_Lexington_and_Concord "Battles of Lexington and Concord") on April 19, 1775.[[46]](#cite_note-Conroy-2000-47) [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") was appointed general of the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army").[[47]](#cite_note-48) Washington's [crossing of the Delaware River](/wiki/George_Washington%27s_crossing_of_the_Delaware_River "George Washington's crossing of the Delaware River") began a series of victories that expelled British forces from New Jersey.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECogliano2009101–102-49) The British began the [Saratoga campaign](/wiki/Saratoga_campaign "Saratoga campaign") in 1777 to capture [Albany, New York](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), as a [choke point](/wiki/Choke_point "Choke point").[[49]](#cite_note-50) After the American [victory at Saratoga](/wiki/Battles_of_Saratoga "Battles of Saratoga"), France, the Netherlands, and Spain began providing support to the Continental Army.[[50]](#cite_note-51) Britain responded to defeat in the [northern theater](/wiki/Northern_theater_of_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Northern theater of the American Revolutionary War") by advancing in the [southern theater](/wiki/Southern_theater_of_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Southern theater of the American Revolutionary War"), beginning with the [capture of Savannah](/wiki/Capture_of_Savannah "Capture of Savannah") in 1778.[[51]](#cite_note-52) American forces reclaimed the south in 1781, and a British army was captured at the [siege of Yorktown](/wiki/Siege_of_Yorktown "Siege of Yorktown") on October 19, 1781.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECogliano2009110-53)

King George III formally ordered the end of hostilities on December 5, 1782, recognizing American independence.[[53]](#cite_note-54) The [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)") was signed on September 3, 1783,[[54]](#cite_note-55) and was ratified by the [Congress of the Confederation](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Confederation "Congress of the Confederation") on January 14, 1784.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

### Confederation period

Main article: [Confederation period](/wiki/Confederation_period "Confederation period")

The [Articles of Confederation](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation "Articles of Confederation") were ratified as the governing law of the United States, written to limit the powers of the central government in favor of states. This caused [economic decline](/wiki/Financial_costs_of_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Financial costs of the American Revolutionary War"), as the government was unable to pass economic legislation, levy taxes, and pay its debts.[[56]](#cite_note-Van_Cleve-2019-57) Nationalists worried that the [confederate](/wiki/Confederation "Confederation") nature of the union was too fragile to withstand an armed conflict with any adversarial states, or even internal revolts such as [Shays' Rebellion](/wiki/Shays%27_Rebellion "Shays' Rebellion") of 1786 in Massachusetts.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenePole2003-58)

In the 1780s the western regions were ceded by the states to Congress and became territories. With the migration of settlers to the Northwest, soon they became [states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state").[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenePole2003-58) The [American Indian Wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars") continued in the 1780s as settlers moved west.[[58]](#cite_note-59) The [Northwestern Confederacy](/wiki/Northwestern_Confederacy "Northwestern Confederacy") and American settlers began fighting the [Northwest Indian War](/wiki/Northwest_Indian_War "Northwest Indian War") in the late 1780s; the Northwestern Confederacy received British support, but the settlers received little assistance from the American government.[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61)

Nationalists – most of them war veterans – organized in every state and convinced Congress to call the [Philadelphia Convention](/wiki/Philadelphia_Convention "Philadelphia Convention") in 1787. The delegates from every state wrote a new [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States") that created a federal government with a strong president and powers of taxation. The new government reflected the prevailing republican ideals of guarantees of [individual liberty](/wiki/Natural_rights_and_legal_rights "Natural rights and legal rights") and of constraining the power of government through [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers").[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenePole2003-58) The constitution was ratified by a sufficient number of states in 1788 to begin forming a federal government.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

## Early republic (1793–1830)

Main article: [History of the United States (1789–1815)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1789%E2%80%931815) "History of the United States (1789–1815)")

The [United States Electoral College](/wiki/United_States_Electoral_College "United States Electoral College") chose George Washington as the first [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") in 1789.[[62]](#cite_note-63) The national capital moved from New York to Philadelphia in 1790 and finally, in 1800, to Washington, D.C.

The major accomplishment of the [Washington Administration](/wiki/Washington_Administration "Washington Administration") was the creation of a strong national government that was recognized by all Americans.[[63]](#cite_note-64) Washington's government, following the vigorous leadership of Treasury Secretary [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"), assumed the debts of the states, created the [Bank of the United States](/wiki/First_Bank_of_the_United_States "First Bank of the United States"), and set up a uniform system of [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff") and other taxes to pay off the debt and provide a financial infrastructure. Hamilton created the [Federalist Party](/wiki/Federalist_Party "Federalist Party") to support his programs. In 1791, to assuage the [Anti-Federalists](/wiki/Anti-Federalists "Anti-Federalists") who feared a too-powerful central government, the Congress adopted the [United States Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights") by amending it to the U.S. Constitution, to guarantee individual liberties such as freedom of speech and religious practice.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

[Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") and [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison") formed an opposition Republican Party (usually called the [Democratic-Republican Party](/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party")). In 1794, Hamilton and Washington presented the country with the [Jay Treaty](/wiki/Jay_Treaty "Jay Treaty") which re-established good relations with Britain. The Jeffersonians vehemently protested, and the voters aligned behind one party or the other, thus setting up the [First Party System](/wiki/First_Party_System "First Party System").[[65]](#cite_note-in_JSTOR-66) Although the treaty passed, politics became intensely heated.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller-67) Serious challenges to the new federal government included the [Northwest Indian War](/wiki/Northwest_Indian_War "Northwest Indian War"), the ongoing [Cherokee–American wars](/wiki/Cherokee%E2%80%93American_wars "Cherokee–American wars"), and the 1794 [Whiskey Rebellion](/wiki/Whiskey_Rebellion "Whiskey Rebellion"), in which western settlers protested against a federal tax on liquor.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELesson_Plan_on_Washington-68)

Washington refused to serve more than two terms, setting a precedent for future administrations.[[68]](#cite_note-69) [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") of the Federalist Party defeated Jefferson in the [1796 election](/wiki/1796_United_States_presidential_election "1796 United States presidential election"). War was looming with France, and the Federalists used the opportunity to try silencing the Republicans with the [Alien and Sedition Acts](/wiki/Alien_and_Sedition_Acts "Alien and Sedition Acts"); they also built up a large army with Hamilton at the head, in preparation for a French invasion. However, the Federalists became divided after Adams sent a successful peace mission to France that ended the [Quasi-War](/wiki/Quasi-War "Quasi-War") of 1798.[[65]](#cite_note-in_JSTOR-66)[[69]](#cite_note-70)

### Increasing demand for slave labor

Main article: [Slavery in the United States](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Crowe-Slaves_Waiting_for_Sale_-_Richmond%2C_Virginia.jpg/250px-Crowe-Slaves_Waiting_for_Sale_-_Richmond%2C_Virginia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crowe-Slaves_Waiting_for_Sale_-_Richmond,_Virginia.jpg)

*Slaves Waiting for Sale: Richmond, Virginia*, an 1861 portrait by British painter [Eyre Crowe](/wiki/Eyre_Crowe_(painter) "Eyre Crowe (painter)")

During the first two decades after the Revolutionary War, there were dramatic changes in the status of slavery among the states and an increase in the number of [free blacks](/wiki/Free_blacks "Free blacks"). Inspired by revolutionary ideals of equality, and influenced by their reduced economic reliance on the use of slaves, the [Northern states](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States") abolished slavery.

States of the [Upper South](/wiki/Upper_South "Upper South") made [manumission](/wiki/Manumission "Manumission") easier, resulting in an increase in the proportion of free blacks in the Upper South (as a percentage of the total non-white population) from less than one percent in 1792 to more than ten percent by 1810. By that date, a total of 13.5 percent of all blacks in the United States were free.[[70]](#cite_note-71) In 1807, with four million slaves already in the United States, Congress severed U.S. involvement with the [Atlantic slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade").[[71]](#cite_note-pbs-72)

### Second Great Awakening

Main article: [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Camp_meeting.jpg/250px-Camp_meeting.jpg)](/wiki/File:Camp_meeting.jpg)

A drawing of a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") camp meeting, c. 1829

The Second Great Awakening was a Protestant revival movement that affected virtually all of society during the early 19th century and led to rapid church growth. It began around 1790 and was gaining momentum by 1800; its membership rose rapidly after 1820 among [Baptist](/wiki/Baptists_in_the_United_States "Baptists in the United States") and [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") congregations, whose preachers led the movement. It was past its peak by the 1840s.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

The movement enrolled millions of new members in existing evangelical denominations and led to the formation of new denominations. The Second Great Awakening stimulated the establishment of many reform movements, including [abolitionism](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") and [temperance](/wiki/Temperance_movement "Temperance movement").[[73]](#cite_note-74)

### Louisiana and Jeffersonian republicanism

Main articles: [Presidency of Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Presidency_of_Thomas_Jefferson "Presidency of Thomas Jefferson") and [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/U.S._Territorial_Acquisitions.png/250px-U.S._Territorial_Acquisitions.png)](/wiki/File:U.S._Territorial_Acquisitions.png)

Land acquired in the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") in 1803, highlighted in white

Thomas Jefferson heavily defeated John Adams in the [1800 presidential election](/wiki/1800_United_States_presidential_election "1800 United States presidential election"). Jefferson's major achievement as president was the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") in 1803, which provided U.S. settlers with vast potential for expansion west of the Mississippi River.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood,_Empire_of_Liberty368–374-75) He supported expeditions to explore and map the new domain, most notably the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition").[[75]](#cite_note-76)

Jefferson believed deeply in [republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism_in_the_United_States "Republicanism in the United States") and advocated that it should be based on the independent [yeoman](/wiki/Yeoman "Yeoman") farmer and planter; he distrusted cities, factories and banks. He also distrusted the federal government and judges, and he tried to weaken the judiciary. Although the Constitution specified a [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"), its functions were vague until [John Marshall](/wiki/John_Marshall "John Marshall"), the [Chief Justice of the United States](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") (1801‍–‍1835), defined them – especially the power to overturn acts of Congress or states that violated the Constitution, first enunciated in 1803 in the *[Marbury v. Madison](/wiki/Marbury_v._Madison "Marbury v. Madison")* case.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

### War of 1812

Main article: [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812")

See also: [History of the United States (1789–1815)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1789%E2%80%931815) "History of the United States (1789–1815)")

During the early 19th century, Anglo-American relations deteriorated over a variety of reasons. British authorities begun seizing American merchant ships trading with France and its allies, which intensified with the 1807 [Orders in Council](/wiki/Orders_in_Council_(1807) "Orders in Council (1807)"). Congress had passed the [Embargo Act of 1807](/wiki/Embargo_Act_of_1807 "Embargo Act of 1807") in an unsuccessful attempt to coerce Britain to rescind the Orders in Council, which ultimately hurt the American economy more than the British. The [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), angered by thousands of its sailors deserting and finding work on American merchantmen, began stopping U.S. ships and reclaiming alleged deserters. Britain also provided support to Native Americans resisting expansion by U.S. colonizers, with the aim of forming an [Indian barrier state](/wiki/Indian_barrier_state "Indian barrier state") which would halt American expansionism. Many American politicians also wished to occupy Canada, either as a bargaining chip for Anglo-American negotiations or to retain it as part of the US.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStagg,_Madison's_War4-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) Despite strong opposition from the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), especially from Federalists who did not want to disrupt trade with Britain, Congress [declared war on the United Kingdom](/wiki/United_States_declaration_of_war_on_the_United_Kingdom "United States declaration of war on the United Kingdom") on June 18, 1812.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood,_Empire_of_Liberty660–672-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Battle_of_Lake_Erie.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Lake_Erie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Lake_Erie.jpg)

After the [Battle of Lake Erie](/wiki/Battle_of_Lake_Erie "Battle of Lake Erie"), [Oliver Hazard Perry](/wiki/Oliver_Hazard_Perry "Oliver Hazard Perry")'s message to [William Henry Harrison](/wiki/William_Henry_Harrison "William Henry Harrison") began with: "We have met the enemy and they are ours", depicted in a 1865 painting by [William H. Powell](/wiki/William_Henry_Powell_(artist) "William Henry Powell (artist)").[[83]](#cite_note-84)

Both sides tried to invade the other and were repelled. The American militia proved ineffective because the soldiers were reluctant to leave home, and multiple American invasions of Canada were repulsed. British blockades ruined American commerce, bankrupted the Treasury, and further angered New Englanders who began smuggling supplies to Britain. Under General [William Henry Harrison](/wiki/William_Henry_Harrison "William Henry Harrison"), the Americans eventually [gained naval control of Lake Erie](/wiki/Battle_of_Lake_Erie "Battle of Lake Erie") and defeated the Indians under [Tecumseh](/wiki/Tecumseh "Tecumseh") in Canada,[[84]](#cite_note-85) while [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") ended the Indian threat in the Southeast. The Indian threat to expansion into the Midwest was permanently ended. The British invaded and occupied much of Maine.

In 1814, the British [raided and burned Washington](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington") but were [pushed back at Baltimore](/wiki/Battle_of_Baltimore "Battle of Baltimore"), where "[The Star-Spangled Banner](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner")" was written to celebrate the American success.
