In upstate New York, a major British invasion of New York State was repelled at the [Battle of Plattsburgh](/wiki/Battle_of_Plattsburgh "Battle of Plattsburgh"), and Jackson decisively defeated another invasion at the [Battle of New Orleans](/wiki/Battle_of_New_Orleans "Battle of New Orleans") in early 1815.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStagg,_War_of_1812152–153-86) The battle at New Orleans had occurred after peace had been signed between American and Britain in the [Treaty of Ghent](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ghent "Treaty of Ghent"), but news of the treaty had not reached Louisiana before the battle occurred. Though the war had ended inconclusively and the U.S. had achieved few of war aims laid out in 1812, Jackson's victory at New Orleans led pro-war Americans to call it a "second war of independence"; the War of 1812 contributed to an emerging American identity that cemented [national pride](/wiki/National_pride "National pride") over state pride.[[86]](#cite_note-87) The war also dispelled America's negative perception of a [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") as opposed to ill-equipped and poorly trained militias.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

### Era of Good Feelings

Main article: [Era of Good Feelings](/wiki/Era_of_Good_Feelings "Era of Good Feelings")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Election_Day_1815_by_John_Lewis_Krimmel.jpg/250px-Election_Day_1815_by_John_Lewis_Krimmel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Election_Day_1815_by_John_Lewis_Krimmel.jpg)

Depiction of election-day activities in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") by [John Lewis Krimmel](/wiki/John_Lewis_Krimmel "John Lewis Krimmel") in 1815

National euphoria after the victory at [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") ruined the prestige of the Federalists, and they no longer played a significant role as a political party.[[88]](#cite_note-89) President Madison and most of the Republicans realized they had been foolish to allow the [First Bank of the United States](/wiki/First_Bank_of_the_United_States "First Bank of the United States") to close down, for its absence had greatly hindered the financing of the war. With the assistance of foreign bankers, they chartered the [Second Bank of the United States](/wiki/Second_Bank_of_the_United_States "Second Bank of the United States") in 1816.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDangerfield-90)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodman56–89-91)

The Republicans also imposed tariffs designed to protect the infant industries that had been created when Britain was blockading the U.S. With the collapse of the Federalists as a party, the adoption of many Federalist principles by the Republicans, and the systematic policy of President [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe") in his two terms (1817‍–‍1825) to downplay partisanship, society entered an [Era of Good Feelings](/wiki/Era_of_Good_Feelings "Era of Good Feelings") and closed out the First Party System.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDangerfield-90)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodman56–89-91)

Expressed in 1823, the [Monroe Doctrine](/wiki/Monroe_Doctrine "Monroe Doctrine") proclaimed the idealistic standpoint of the United States that European powers should no longer colonize or interfere in the Americas. This was a defining moment in [U.S. foreign policy](/wiki/U.S._foreign_policy "U.S. foreign policy").[[91]](#cite_note-92)

In 1832, President Andrew Jackson ran for a second term under the slogan "Jackson and no bank" and did not renew the Second Bank's charter, dissolving the bank in 1836.[[92]](#cite_note-North_Carolina_History-93) He was convinced that central banking was used by the elite to take advantage of the average American, and he opted instead to implement publicly owned banks in various U.S. states, popularly known as "pet banks".[[92]](#cite_note-North_Carolina_History-93)

## Expansion and reform (1830–1848)

Main article: [History of the United States (1815–1849)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1815%E2%80%931849) "History of the United States (1815–1849)")

### Second Party System

Main articles: [Second Party System](/wiki/Second_Party_System "Second Party System") and [Presidency of Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Presidency_of_Andrew_Jackson "Presidency of Andrew Jackson")

The former Jeffersonian (Democratic-Republican) party split into factions over the choice of a successor to President [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe"), and the party faction that supported many of the old Jeffersonian principles, led by [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") and [Martin Van Buren](/wiki/Martin_Van_Buren "Martin Van Buren"), became the Democratic Party. As Norton explains the transformation in 1828:

> Jacksonians believed the people's will had finally prevailed. Through a lavishly financed coalition of state parties, political leaders, and newspaper editors, a popular movement had elected the president. The Democrats became the nation's first well-organized national party, and tight party organization became the hallmark of nineteenth-century American politics.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorton_et_al.-94)

Opposing factions led by [Henry Clay](/wiki/Henry_Clay "Henry Clay") helped form the [Whig Party](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)"). The Democratic Party had a small but decisive advantage over the [Whigs](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)") until the 1850s, when the Whigs fell apart over the issue of slavery.

### Westward expansion and manifest destiny

Main articles: [American frontier](/wiki/American_frontier "American frontier"), [Manifest destiny](/wiki/Manifest_destiny "Manifest destiny"), and [Indian removal](/wiki/Indian_removal "Indian removal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Trails_of_Tears_en.png/250px-Trails_of_Tears_en.png)](/wiki/File:Trails_of_Tears_en.png)

The Indian Removal Act resulted in the transplantation of several [Native American tribes](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") and the [Trail of Tears](/wiki/Trail_of_Tears "Trail of Tears")

The country grew rapidly in population and area, as pioneers pushed the frontier of settlement west.[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96) Native American tribes in some places resisted militarily, but they were overwhelmed by settlers and the army, and after 1830, were relocated to reservations in the west.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHine_and_Faragher,_American_West10-97) That year, Congress passed the [Indian Removal Act](/wiki/Indian_Removal_Act "Indian Removal Act"), which authorized the president to negotiate treaties that exchanged Native American tribal lands in the eastern states for lands west of the Mississippi River.[[97]](#cite_note-98) Its goal was primarily to remove Native Americans, including the [Five Civilized Tribes](/wiki/Five_Civilized_Tribes "Five Civilized Tribes"), from desirable lands in what's now [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma").[[98]](#cite_note-Remini-2002-99) Thousands of deaths resulted from the relocations, as seen in the Cherokee [Trail of Tears](/wiki/Trail_of_Tears "Trail of Tears"),[[98]](#cite_note-Remini-2002-99) most infamously of which resulted in approximately 4,000 of the 16,000 relocated [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee "Cherokee") dying along the way in 1838.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStannard1993124-100)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThornton75–93-101) Many of the [Seminole Indians](/wiki/Seminole_Indians "Seminole Indians") in Florida refused to move west, and fought the Army for years in the [Seminole Wars](/wiki/Seminole_Wars "Seminole Wars").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/1850_Woman_and_Men_in_California_Gold_Rush.jpg/250px-1850_Woman_and_Men_in_California_Gold_Rush.jpg)](/wiki/File:1850_Woman_and_Men_in_California_Gold_Rush.jpg)

During the [California Gold Rush](/wiki/California_Gold_Rush "California Gold Rush"), some 300,000 people relocated to [California](/wiki/California "California") from the rest of the United States and abroad following the discovery of gold in the state.

[Manifest destiny](/wiki/Manifest_destiny "Manifest destiny") was the belief that American settlers were destined to expand across the continent.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcPherson198841–46-102) Manifest destiny was rejected by modernizers, especially the Whigs like [Henry Clay](/wiki/Henry_Clay "Henry Clay") and [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") who wanted to build cities and factories – not more farms.[[b]](#cite_note-104) [Democrats](/wiki/History_of_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "History of the Democratic Party (United States)") strongly favored expansion, and won the key [election of 1844](/wiki/1844_United_States_presidential_election "1844 United States presidential election"). After a bitter debate in Congress, the [Republic of Texas was annexed](/wiki/Texas_annexation "Texas annexation") in 1845, leading to the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHine_and_Faragher,_American_West-105) The U.S. Army invaded Mexico at several points, [captured Mexico City](/wiki/Battle_for_Mexico_City "Battle for Mexico City"), and won the war decisively.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowe798-103)

The [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") ended the war in 1848. In the treaty, the U.S. acquired much of the [American Southwest](/wiki/American_Southwest "American Southwest"), including [California](/wiki/California "California") and [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), with Mexico being paid $15 million in compensation. Many Democrats wanted to annex all of Mexico, but that idea was rejected by [White Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners"), who argued that incorporating millions of [Mexican people](/wiki/Mexican_people "Mexican people"), mainly of mixed race, would undermine the U.S. as an exclusively white republic.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowe798-103) Instead, the [U.S. took Texas](/wiki/Texas_annexation "Texas annexation") and the lightly settled northern parts (California and New Mexico). Simultaneously, gold was discovered in California in 1848, and led to over 100,000 white settlers flocking to California in 1849, the [California Gold Rush](/wiki/California_Gold_Rush "California Gold Rush") miners, known as '49ers. [California](/wiki/California "California") grew rapidly, and by 1850, the non-native population went from 1,000 to over 125,000 people by 1850. However, many Indians were devastated by the rapid influx of the rush of white settlers in the 1850s, and to clear the state for white settlement, the U.S. government began a [policy of forcibly removing and exterminating the natives](/wiki/California_genocide "California genocide").[[104]](#cite_note-106) From the early 1830s to 1869, the [Oregon Trail](/wiki/Oregon_Trail "Oregon Trail") and its offshoots were used by over 300,000 settlers headed to California, [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), and other points in the far west. [Wagon trains](/wiki/Wagon_train "Wagon train") took five or six months on foot.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

A peaceful compromise with Britain gave the U.S. ownership of the [Oregon Country](/wiki/Oregon_Country "Oregon Country"), which was renamed the [Oregon Territory](/wiki/Oregon_Territory "Oregon Territory").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHine_and_Faragher,_American_West-105) The demand for [guano](/wiki/Guano "Guano"), prized as an agricultural [fertilizer](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer"), led the U.S. to pass the [Guano Islands Act](/wiki/Guano_Islands_Act "Guano Islands Act") in 1856, which enabled U.S. citizens to take possession, in the name of the country, of unclaimed islands containing guano deposits. Under the act, the U.S. annexed nearly 100 islands in the Pacific Ocean and the Caribbean Sea. By 1903, 66 of these islands were recognized as U.S. territories.[[106]](#cite_note-108)

The [women's suffrage movement](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_the_United_States "Women's suffrage in the United States") began with the [Seneca Falls Convention](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_Convention "Seneca Falls Convention") in 1848. It was organized by women's rights advocates and abolitionists, [Elizabeth Cady Stanton](/wiki/Elizabeth_Cady_Stanton "Elizabeth Cady Stanton") and [Lucretia Mott](/wiki/Lucretia_Mott "Lucretia Mott"). The convention began on July 19, 1848, in the town of [Seneca Falls, New York](/wiki/Seneca_Falls,_New_York "Seneca Falls, New York"), with 300 people in attendance, and concluded the next day with signing the [Declaration of Sentiments](/wiki/Declaration_of_Sentiments "Declaration of Sentiments") demanding equal rights for women, including the right to vote.[[c]](#cite_note-110) The women's rights campaign during [first-wave feminism](/wiki/First-wave_feminism "First-wave feminism") was led by [Elizabeth Cady Stanton](/wiki/Elizabeth_Cady_Stanton "Elizabeth Cady Stanton"), [Lucy Stone](/wiki/Lucy_Stone "Lucy Stone") and [Susan B. Anthony](/wiki/Susan_B._Anthony "Susan B. Anthony"), among others. Stone and [Paulina Wright Davis](/wiki/Paulina_Wright_Davis "Paulina Wright Davis") organized the prominent and influential [National Women's Rights Convention](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Rights_Convention "National Women's Rights Convention") in 1850.[[108]](#cite_note-Mead-2006-111)

## Civil War and Reconstruction (1848–1877)

Main articles: [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), [Reconstruction era](/wiki/Reconstruction_era "Reconstruction era"), and [Greater Reconstruction](/wiki/Greater_Reconstruction "Greater Reconstruction")

### Divisions between North and South

Main articles: [Origins of the American Civil War](/wiki/Origins_of_the_American_Civil_War "Origins of the American Civil War"), [History of the United States (1849–1865)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1849%E2%80%931865) "History of the United States (1849–1865)"), and [Abolitionism in the United States](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States")

See also: [Plantation complexes in the Southern United States](/wiki/Plantation_complexes_in_the_Southern_United_States "Plantation complexes in the Southern United States"), [Proslavery thought](/wiki/Proslavery_thought "Proslavery thought"), and [Antebellum South](/wiki/Antebellum_South "Antebellum South")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/US_Secession_map_1863_%28BlankMap_derived%29.png/330px-US_Secession_map_1863_%28BlankMap_derived%29.png)](/wiki/File:US_Secession_map_1863_(BlankMap_derived).png)

An 1863 map of the United States during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), showing the affiliation of states and territories

Union states

Union territories not permitting slavery

Border Union states, permitting slavery

Confederate states

Union territories permitting slavery (claimed by Confederacy)

The central issue after 1848 was the expansion of slavery, with the anti-slavery elements in the North pitted against the pro-slavery elements in the South.[[109]](#cite_note-Forret-2012-112) By 1860, there were four million slaves in the South. A small number of Northerners called abolitionists [sought the immediate abolition of slavery](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States"), while much larger numbers in the North were opposed to the expansion of slavery and sought to put it on the path to extinction.[[109]](#cite_note-Forret-2012-112)

There was resistance to slavery by both peaceful and violent means. [Slave rebellions](/wiki/Slave_rebellion_and_resistance_in_the_United_States "Slave rebellion and resistance in the United States") by [Gabriel Prosser](/wiki/Gabriel_Prosser "Gabriel Prosser") (1800), [Denmark Vesey](/wiki/Denmark_Vesey "Denmark Vesey") (1822) and [Nat Turner](/wiki/Nat_Turner%27s_Rebellion "Nat Turner's Rebellion") (1831), caused fear in the white South, where stricter oversight of slaves was imposed, and the rights of free Black people were reduced.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Southern white Democrats](/wiki/Southern_Democrats "Southern Democrats") insisted that slavery was of economic, social, and cultural benefit, even to the slaves themselves.[[109]](#cite_note-Forret-2012-112) Supporters of slavery argued that a sudden end to the slave economy would have a fatal economic impact in the South, causing widespread unemployment and chaos; slave labor was the foundation of their economy.[[110]](#cite_note-113)

The [plantations](/wiki/Plantation_complexes_in_the_Southern_United_States "Plantation complexes in the Southern United States") were highly profitable because of the heavy European demand for raw cotton. Northern cities and regional industries were tied economically to slavery through banking, shipping, and manufacturing, including their [textile mills](/wiki/Textile_mills "Textile mills"). In addition, Southern states benefited from slavery by having an increased apportionment in Congress due to the partial counting of slaves in their populations.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Remember_Your_Weekly_Pledge_Massachusetts_Anti-Slavey_Society_collection_box.jpg/250px-Remember_Your_Weekly_Pledge_Massachusetts_Anti-Slavey_Society_collection_box.jpg)](/wiki/File:Remember_Your_Weekly_Pledge_Massachusetts_Anti-Slavey_Society_collection_box.jpg)

*Remember Your Weekly Pledge*, a collection box for the [Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society](/wiki/Massachusetts_Anti-Slavery_Society "Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society") in 1850

The issue of slavery in the new territories was seemingly settled by the [Compromise of 1850](/wiki/Compromise_of_1850 "Compromise of 1850"), which included the admission of California as a [free state](/wiki/Slave_states_and_free_states "Slave states and free states") in exchange for no federal restrictions on slavery placed on Utah or New Mexico.[[111]](#cite_note-114) A point of contention was the [Fugitive Slave Act](/wiki/Fugitive_Slave_Act "Fugitive Slave Act"), requiring free states to cooperate with slave owners when attempting to capture escaped slaves who defected via the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad"), while punishing those who aided runaway slaves. The act garnered outrage in the North, as they were forced to play a role in Southern slavery.[[112]](#cite_note-Rotunda-1992-115)[[113]](#cite_note-APP-1860-116)[[114]](#cite_note-Bordewich-2012-117)

The Missouri Compromise was repealed in 1854 with the [Kansas–Nebraska Act](/wiki/Kansas%E2%80%93Nebraska_Act "Kansas–Nebraska Act"); promoted by Stephen Douglas in the name of "[popular sovereignty](/wiki/Popular_sovereignty_in_the_United_States "Popular sovereignty in the United States")" and democracy, this act of Congress permitted voters to decide on the legality of slavery in each territory, and repealed the [Missouri Compromise of 1820](/wiki/Missouri_Compromise_of_1820 "Missouri Compromise of 1820"), which explicitly banned slavery in this territory. Anti-slavery forces rose in anger and alarm, forming the new [Republican Party](/wiki/History_of_the_Republican_Party_(United_States) "History of the Republican Party (United States)") to oppose the expansion of slavery out west. Pro- and anti- slavery settlers rushed to Kansas to vote for or against slavery, resulting in years of bloodshed and violence called [Bleeding Kansas](/wiki/Bleeding_Kansas "Bleeding Kansas"). By the late 1850s, the young Republican Party dominated nearly all Northern states, and hence the electoral colleges. The party insisted that slavery would never be allowed to expand and would therefore slowly die out.[[115]](#cite_note-118)

The Supreme Court's 1857 decision in *[Dred Scott v. Sandford](/wiki/Dred_Scott_v._Sandford "Dred Scott v. Sandford")* ruled that the Compromise was unconstitutional, and that free Black people were not U.S. citizens, and therefore unable to sue for their freedom. The decision enraged Northerners, and the Republicans worried that the decision could be used to expand slavery. The decision exacerbated tensions over slavery.[[112]](#cite_note-Rotunda-1992-115)[[113]](#cite_note-APP-1860-116)[[114]](#cite_note-Bordewich-2012-117)

### Civil War

Main articles: [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), [Confederate States of America](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America"), and [1860 United States presidential election](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election "1860 United States presidential election")

See also: [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army"), [Confederate States Army](/wiki/Confederate_States_Army "Confederate States Army"), [Military history of African Americans in the American Civil War](/wiki/Military_history_of_African_Americans_in_the_American_Civil_War "Military history of African Americans in the American Civil War"), [End of the American Civil War](/wiki/End_of_the_American_Civil_War "End of the American Civil War"), and [End of slavery in the United States](/wiki/End_of_slavery_in_the_United_States "End of slavery in the United States")

After Republican [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") won the [1860 election](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election "1860 United States presidential election"), seven Southern states [seceded](/wiki/Seceded "Seceded") from the Union and formed the [Confederate States of America](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") (Confederacy) on February 8, 1861, in order to persevere the institution of slavery.[[116]](#cite_note-119) The [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") began on April 12, 1861, when [Confederate forces attacked a U.S. military installation at Fort Sumter](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Sumter "Battle of Fort Sumter") in South Carolina. In response, Lincoln [called on the states to send militiamen](/wiki/Proclamation_80 "Proclamation 80") to recapture forts, protect Washington D.C., and "preserve the Union".[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuelzo,_Fateful_Lightning-120) Lincoln's call led to four more states seceding and joining the Confederacy. A few of the (northernmost) slave states did not secede and became known as the [border states](/wiki/Border_states_(American_Civil_War) "Border states (American Civil War)"); these were Delaware, Maryland, Kentucky, and Missouri.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] During the war, the northwestern portion of Virginia seceded from the Confederacy, becoming the new Union state of [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia").[[118]](#cite_note-121)

The two armies' first major battle was the [First Battle of Bull Run](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Bull_Run "First Battle of Bull Run"), which the Confederate victory proved to both sides that the war would be much longer than anticipated.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuelzo,_Fateful_Lightning-120) In the [western theater](/wiki/Western_Theater_of_the_American_Civil_War "Western Theater of the American Civil War"), the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") was relatively successful, with major battles such as [Perryville](/wiki/Battle_of_Perryville "Battle of Perryville") and [Shiloh](/wiki/Battle_of_Shiloh "Battle of Shiloh"), along with [Union Navy](/wiki/Union_Navy "Union Navy") gunboat dominance of navigable rivers producing strategic Union victories and destroying major Confederate operations.[[119]](#cite_note-122) Warfare in the [eastern theater](/wiki/Eastern_Theater_of_the_American_Civil_War "Eastern Theater of the American Civil War") began poorly for the Union. U.S. General [George B. McClellan](/wiki/George_B._McClellan "George B. McClellan") failed to capture the Confederate capital of [Richmond, Virginia](/wiki/Richmond,_Virginia "Richmond, Virginia"), in his [Peninsula campaign](/wiki/Peninsula_campaign "Peninsula campaign") and [retreated after attacks](/wiki/Seven_Days_Battles "Seven Days Battles") from Confederate General [Robert E. Lee](/wiki/Robert_E._Lee "Robert E. Lee").[[120]](#cite_note-123)

Meanwhile, in 1861 and 1862, both sides concentrated on raising and training new armies. The Union successfully gained control of the border states, driving the Confederates out.[[121]](#cite_note-McPherson-124) Lee's [Army of Northern Virginia](/wiki/Army_of_Northern_Virginia "Army of Northern Virginia") won battles in late 1862 and spring 1863, but he pushed too hard and ignored the Union threat in the west. He [invaded Pennsylvania](/wiki/Gettysburg_campaign "Gettysburg campaign") in search of supplies and to cause [war-weariness](/wiki/War-weariness "War-weariness") in the North. On September 17, 1862, the [Battle of Antietam](/wiki/Battle_of_Antietam "Battle of Antietam") was fought in [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), which would result in over 23,000 casualties in bloodiest day of the Civil War.[[122]](#cite_note-Harris-1863-125)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/General_Robert_E._Lee_surrenders_at_Appomattox_Court_House_1865.jpg/250px-General_Robert_E._Lee_surrenders_at_Appomattox_Court_House_1865.jpg)](/wiki/File:General_Robert_E._Lee_surrenders_at_Appomattox_Court_House_1865.jpg)

A portrait of [Robert E. Lee](/wiki/Robert_E._Lee "Robert E. Lee") and the [Confederate States Army](/wiki/Confederate_States_Army "Confederate States Army") surrendering to the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") following the [Battle of Appomattox Court House](/wiki/Battle_of_Appomattox_Court_House "Battle of Appomattox Court House") in [Appomattox County, Virginia](/wiki/Appomattox_County,_Virginia "Appomattox County, Virginia"), on April 9, 1865

The [Emancipation Proclamation](/wiki/Emancipation_Proclamation "Emancipation Proclamation"), issued by Lincoln on January 1, 1863, freed three million slaves in designated areas of the Confederacy in order to weaken the Confederate's troops and supplies.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuelzo,_Lincoln's_Emancipation_Proclamation-126) In perhaps the [turning point of the war](/wiki/Turning_point_of_the_American_Civil_War "Turning point of the American Civil War"), Lee's army was badly beaten by the [Army of the Potomac](/wiki/Army_of_the_Potomac "Army of the Potomac"), and in July 1863, the [Union](/wiki/Union_army "Union army")'s victory at the [Battle of Gettysburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Gettysburg "Battle of Gettysburg") in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), the [deadliest battle in American military history](/wiki/List_of_battles_with_most_United_States_military_fatalities "List of battles with most United States military fatalities") with over 50,000 casualties, proved a [turning point in the war](/wiki/Turning_point_of_the_American_Civil_War "Turning point of the American Civil War").[[121]](#cite_note-McPherson-124) Survivors of the battle were immediately redeployed to suppress the [New York City draft riots](/wiki/New_York_City_draft_riots "New York City draft riots") by Irish Americans protesting [Civil War conscription](/wiki/Conscription_in_the_United_States "Conscription in the United States") and the city's free Black population.[[122]](#cite_note-Harris-1863-125)

In July 1863, Union forces under General [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") gained control of the Mississippi River at the [Battle of Vicksburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Vicksburg "Battle of Vicksburg"), splitting the Confederacy. In 1864, Union General [William Tecumseh Sherman](/wiki/William_Tecumseh_Sherman "William Tecumseh Sherman") marched south from [Chattanooga](/wiki/Chattanooga "Chattanooga") to capture [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), a decisive victory that ended war jitters among Republicans in the North and helped Lincoln [win re-election](/wiki/1864_United_States_presidential_election "1864 United States presidential election"). [Sherman's march to the sea](/wiki/Sherman%27s_march_to_the_sea "Sherman's march to the sea") was almost unopposed. Much of the South was destroyed, and could no longer provide desperately needed supplies to its armies. In spring 1864, Grant launched a [war of attrition](/wiki/War_of_attrition "War of attrition") and pursued Lee to the final [Appomattox campaign](/wiki/Appomattox_campaign "Appomattox campaign"), which resulted in Lee [surrendering](/wiki/Battle_of_Appomattox_Court_House "Battle of Appomattox Court House") in April 1865, ending the war after four years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] By May 1865, the Union Army controlled all of the Confederacy and liberated all of the designated slaves.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuelzo,_Lincoln's_Emancipation_Proclamation-126)

The Civil War was the world's earliest [industrial war](/wiki/Industrial_warfare "Industrial warfare"). Railroads, the [telegraph](/wiki/Telegraph "Telegraph"), steamships, and mass-produced weapons were employed extensively. Civilian factories, mines, shipyards, and were mobilized.[[124]](#cite_note-Huddleston-2002-127) Foreign trade increased, with the U.S. providing both food and cotton to Britain, and Britain sending in manufactured products and thousands of volunteers for the Union Army, and a few to the [Confederate army](/wiki/Confederate_army "Confederate army"). The Union blockade shut down Confederate ports. It remains the deadliest war in American history, resulting in the deaths of about 750,000 soldiers and an undetermined number of [civilian casualties](/wiki/Civilian_casualties "Civilian casualties").[[d]](#cite_note-130) About ten percent of all Northern males 20–45 years old, and thirty percent of all Southern white males aged 18–40 died.[[124]](#cite_note-Huddleston-2002-127) Many Black people died after being dislocated during the war and Reconstruction.[[127]](#cite_note-131)

### Reconstruction

Main articles: [Reconstruction era](/wiki/Reconstruction_era "Reconstruction era") and [History of the United States (1865–1918)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1865%E2%80%931918) "History of the United States (1865–1918)")

Further information: [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan"), [Jim Crow laws](/wiki/Jim_Crow_laws "Jim Crow laws"), and [Nadir of American race relations](/wiki/Nadir_of_American_race_relations "Nadir of American race relations")

[Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_era "Reconstruction era") followed after the end of the war.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuelzo,_Fateful_Lightning-120)[[128]](#cite_note-132)[[129]](#cite_note-133) Days after the American Civil War ended with the Union victory and the surrender of the [Appatomox Court House](/wiki/Surrender_at_Appomattox "Surrender at Appomattox").[[130]](#cite_note-134) Lincoln was [assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Assassination of Abraham Lincoln") at Ford's Theater in [Washington D.C.](/wiki/Washington_D.C. "Washington D.C.") in April 1865 by [John Wilkes Booth](/wiki/John_Wilkes_Booth "John Wilkes Booth"), and was succeeded by [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson").[[131]](#cite_note-135)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/East_and_West_Shaking_hands_at_the_laying_of_last_rail_Union_Pacific_Railroad_-_Restoration.jpg/250px-East_and_West_Shaking_hands_at_the_laying_of_last_rail_Union_Pacific_Railroad_-_Restoration.jpg)](/wiki/File:East_and_West_Shaking_hands_at_the_laying_of_last_rail_Union_Pacific_Railroad_-_Restoration.jpg)

A May 10, 1869 picture of the completion of the [first transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad") in [Promontory, Utah](/wiki/Promontory,_Utah "Promontory, Utah")

After the war, the far west was developed and settled, first by wagon trains and riverboats, and then by the [first transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad") in 1869. Many Northern European immigrants took up low-cost or free farms in the Prairie States. Mining for silver and copper encouraged development.[[132]](#cite_note-136)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/FreedmenVotingInNewOrleans1867.jpeg/250px-FreedmenVotingInNewOrleans1867.jpeg)](/wiki/File:FreedmenVotingInNewOrleans1867.jpeg)

An illustration of African-American [Freedmen](/wiki/Freedman "Freedman") voting in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") in 1867

The severe threats of starvation and displacement of the unemployed [Freedmen](/wiki/Freedmen "Freedmen") were met by the first major federal relief agency, the [Freedmen's Bureau](/wiki/Freedmen%27s_Bureau "Freedmen's Bureau"), operated by the Army.[[133]](#cite_note-137) The bureau also took in freed slaves.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Three "[Reconstruction Amendments](/wiki/Reconstruction_Amendments "Reconstruction Amendments")" expanded civil rights for black Americans: the 1865 [Thirteenth Amendment](/wiki/Thirteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") outlawed slavery;[[134]](#cite_note-138) the 1868 [Fourteenth Amendment](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") guaranteed equal rights and citizenship for Black people;[[135]](#cite_note-139) the 1870 [Fifteenth Amendment](/wiki/Fifteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fifteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") prevented race from being used to disenfranchise men.[[136]](#cite_note-140)

Ex-Confederates remained in control of most Southern states for over two years, until the [Radical Republicans](/wiki/Radical_Republicans "Radical Republicans") gained control of Congress in the [1866 elections](/wiki/1866_elections "1866 elections"). Johnson, who sought good treatment for ex-Confederates, was virtually powerless in the face of Congress and vetoed many civil rights laws; [he was impeached](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Andrew_Johnson "Impeachment of Andrew Johnson") in 1868, but the Senate's [attempt to remove him](/wiki/Impeachment_trial_of_Andrew_Johnson "Impeachment trial of Andrew Johnson") from office failed by one vote. Congress enfranchised black men and temporarily banned many ex-Confederate leaders from holding office. New Republican governments came to power based on a coalition of Freedmen made up of [Carpetbaggers](/wiki/Carpetbagger "Carpetbagger") (new arrivals from the North), and [Scalawags](/wiki/Scalawag "Scalawag") (native white Southerners), backed by the Army. Opponents said they were corrupt and violated the rights of whites.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERable-141)

During Reconstruction, for a brief period, many freed blacks could vote, and even hold public office as the Republicans occupied the South. In response to Radical Reconstruction, the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") (KKK) emerged in 1865 as a white-supremacist terrorist organization opposed to black civil rights and Republican rule. President Ulysses Grant's enforcement of the [Ku Klux Klan Act](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan_Act "Ku Klux Klan Act") of 1871 shut them down.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERable-141) Paramilitary groups, such as the [White League](/wiki/White_League "White League") and [Red Shirts](/wiki/Red_Shirts_(United_States) "Red Shirts (United States)") emerging around 1874, openly intimidated and attacked Black people voting.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERable-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Kkk-carpetbagger-cartoon.jpg/250px-Kkk-carpetbagger-cartoon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kkk-carpetbagger-cartoon.jpg)

A September 1, 1868 cartoon from [Tuscaloosa](/wiki/Tuscaloosa,_Alabama "Tuscaloosa, Alabama")'s *Independent Monitor*, threatening that the [KKK](/wiki/First_Klan "First Klan") will [lynch](/wiki/Lynching "Lynching") [scalawags](/wiki/Scalawag "Scalawag") (left) and [carpetbaggers](/wiki/Carpetbagger "Carpetbagger") (right) the day [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") takes office in 1869

Reconstruction ended after the disputed [1876 election](/wiki/1876_United_States_presidential_election "1876 United States presidential election"). The [Compromise of 1877](/wiki/Compromise_of_1877 "Compromise of 1877") gave Republican [Rutherford B. Hayes](/wiki/Rutherford_B._Hayes "Rutherford B. Hayes") the presidency in exchange for removing all remaining federal troops in the South, as the Democrats regained control of the Congress, and Reconstruction came to an end in 1877. As a result, the southern states passes harsh laws on African-Americans known as [Jim Crow laws](/wiki/Jim_Crow_laws "Jim Crow laws"), that effectively [disenfranchised](/wiki/Disenfranchisement_after_the_Reconstruction_era "Disenfranchisement after the Reconstruction era") Black and poor white voters by making voter registration more difficult through [poll taxes](/wiki/Poll_taxes_in_the_United_States "Poll taxes in the United States") and [literacy tests](/wiki/Literacy_test "Literacy test"). Black people were segregated from whites in the violently enforced Jim Crow system, and lynchings and threats of violence against blacks were commonplace.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVann_Woodward-142)[[139]](#cite_note-143)[[140]](#cite_note-144) In 1882, the United States passed the [Chinese Exclusion Act](/wiki/Chinese_Exclusion_Act "Chinese Exclusion Act") (which barred all Chinese immigrants except for students and businessmen),[[141]](#cite_note-Anderson_Diplomacy-145) and the [Immigration Act of 1882](/wiki/Immigration_Act_of_1882 "Immigration Act of 1882") (which barred all immigrants with mental health issues).[[142]](#cite_note-146)

### Railroads

The railroad produced as profound a change in the 1800s as electricity did in the 20th century. As historian Chester Wright put it, "Its revolutionizing effects can scarcely be exaggerated."[[143]](#cite_note-147) The [first transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad") cut the time it took to get across the country from months down to days. Even more importantly, it cut freight rates down to a small fraction of what they had been with wagon transport.[[144]](#cite_note-148)[[145]](#cite_note-149)[[146]](#cite_note-150)

Prior to the Civil War, average rail freight rates were 3 cents per ton-mile (A “ton-mile” is one ton of freight hauled one mile.), compared to 15 cents by wagon. Rail freight rates fell even further, and were less than 1 cent per ton-mile by 1895. For example, a farmer in the West might have a load of corn that was worth $100 in Chicago, but it would cost $100 to get it there by wagon. So, farming wasn't profitable in most of the West. The coming of the railroad changed this – dramatically. Instantaneously, freight rates dropped to a small fraction of what they had been by wagon. As a result, farming throughout the West could be done at a profit. So could mining, logging and ranching.[[147]](#cite_note-151)[[148]](#cite_note-152)[[149]](#cite_note-153)[[150]](#cite_note-154)[[151]](#cite_note-155)

All forms of economic activity increased significantly in the 1800s as a result of low-cost rail transportation. As historian George Rogers Taylor put it, “The magnitude of this change was so great as to permit a major revolution in domestic commerce.”[[152]](#cite_note-156)[[153]](#cite_note-157)

When [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") bought the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") in 1803, people thought it would take 300 years to populate it. With the coming of the railroad, it was accomplished in only 30 years.[[154]](#cite_note-158)[[155]](#cite_note-159)

## Gilded Age and the Progressive Era (1877–1914)

Main articles: [Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age"), [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era"), and [Second Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Second_Industrial_Revolution "Second Industrial Revolution")

### After Reconstruction

The "[Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age")" was a term that [Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain") used to describe the period of the late 19th century with a dramatic expansion of American wealth and prosperity, underscored by mass corruption in government.[[156]](#cite_note-Trachtenberg-2007-160) Some historians have argued that the United States was effectively [plutocratic](/wiki/Plutocratic "Plutocratic") for at least part of the era.[[157]](#cite_note-161)[[158]](#cite_note-162)[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeard-163) As financiers and industrialists such as [J.P. Morgan](/wiki/J.P._Morgan "J.P. Morgan") and [John D. Rockefeller](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller "John D. Rockefeller") began to amass vast fortunes, many observers were concerned that the nation was losing its pioneering egalitarian spirit.[[160]](#cite_note-164)

An unprecedented wave of [immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") from Europe served to both provide the labor for American industry and create diverse communities in previously undeveloped areas. From 1880 to 1914, peak years of immigration, more than 22 million people migrated to the country.[[161]](#cite_note-165) Most were unskilled workers who quickly found jobs in mines, mills, and factories. Many immigrants were craftsmen and farmers who purchased inexpensive land on the prairies. Poverty, growing inequality and dangerous working conditions, along with [socialist](/wiki/Socialism_in_the_United_States "Socialism in the United States") and [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchism_in_the_United_States "Anarchism in the United States") ideas diffusing from European immigrants, led to the rise of the [labor movement](/wiki/Labor_history_of_the_United_States "Labor history of the United States").[[162]](#cite_note-166)[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZinn264–282-167)[[164]](#cite_note-168)

Dissatisfaction on the part of the growing middle class with the corruption and inefficiency of politics, and the failure to deal with increasingly important urban and industrial problems, led to the dynamic [progressive movement](/wiki/Progressive_movement "Progressive movement") starting in the 1890s. Progressives called for the modernization and reform of decrepit institutions in the fields of politics, education, medicine, and industry.[[165]](#cite_note-digital_history_progressive-169) ["Muckraking" journalists](/wiki/Muckraker "Muckraker") exposed corruption in business and government, and highlighted rampant inner-city poverty. Progressives implemented antitrust laws and regulated such industries of meatpacking, drugs, and railroads. Four new constitutional amendments – the [Sixteenth](/wiki/Sixteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Sixteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") through [Nineteenth](/wiki/Nineteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") – resulted from progressive activism, bringing the [federal income tax](/wiki/Federal_income_tax "Federal income tax"), direct election of Senators, prohibition, and female suffrage.[[165]](#cite_note-digital_history_progressive-169)

In 1881, President [James A. Garfield](/wiki/James_A._Garfield "James A. Garfield") was assassinated by [Charles Guiteau](/wiki/Charles_Guiteau "Charles Guiteau").[[166]](#cite_note-170)

#### Unions and strikes

Main articles: [Labor history of the United States](/wiki/Labor_history_of_the_United_States "Labor history of the United States") and [Union violence in the United States](/wiki/Union_violence_in_the_United_States "Union violence in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/47/940721-remington-givingthemthebutt-harpersweekly.jpg/250px-940721-remington-givingthemthebutt-harpersweekly.jpg)](/wiki/File:940721-remington-givingthemthebutt-harpersweekly.jpg)

A *[Harpers Weekly](/wiki/Harpers_Weekly "Harpers Weekly")* illustration by [Frederic Remington](/wiki/Frederic_Remington "Frederic Remington") depicting hundreds of boxcars and coal cars looted and burned and state and federal troops violently attacked striking workers on July 7, 1894

Skilled workers banded together to control their crafts and raise wages by forming labor unions in industrial areas of the Northeast. [Samuel Gompers](/wiki/Samuel_Gompers "Samuel Gompers") led the [American Federation of Labor](/wiki/American_Federation_of_Labor "American Federation of Labor") (1886–1924), coordinating multiple unions. In response to heavy debts and decreasing farm prices, wheat and cotton farmers joined the [Populist Party](/wiki/People%27s_Party_(United_States) "People's Party (United States)").[[167]](#cite_note-171)

The [Panic of 1893](/wiki/Panic_of_1893 "Panic of 1893") created a severe nationwide depression.[[168]](#cite_note-172) Many railroads went bankrupt. Labor unrest involved numerous strikes, most notably the violent [Pullman Strike](/wiki/Pullman_Strike "Pullman Strike") of 1894, which was forcibly shut down by federal troops. One of the disillusioned leaders of the Pullman strike, [Eugene V. Debs](/wiki/Eugene_V._Debs "Eugene V. Debs"), went on to become the leader of the [Socialist Party of America](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_America "Socialist Party of America").[[169]](#cite_note-173)

#### Economic growth

Important legislation of the era included the 1883 [Civil Service Act](/wiki/Pendleton_Civil_Service_Reform_Act "Pendleton Civil Service Reform Act"), which mandated a competitive examination for applicants for government jobs, the 1887 [Interstate Commerce Act](/wiki/Interstate_Commerce_Act "Interstate Commerce Act"), which ended railroads' discrimination against small shippers, and the 1890 [Sherman Antitrust Act](/wiki/Sherman_Antitrust_Act "Sherman Antitrust Act"), which outlawed monopolies in business.[[156]](#cite_note-Trachtenberg-2007-160)

After 1893, the Populist Party gained strength among farmers and coal miners, but was overtaken by the even more popular [Free silver](/wiki/Free_silver "Free silver") movement, which demanded using silver to enlarge the money supply and end the depression.[[170]](#cite_note-174) Financial and railroad communities fought back hard, arguing that only the [gold standard](/wiki/Gold_standard "Gold standard") would save the economy. In the [1896 presidential election](/wiki/1896_United_States_presidential_election "1896 United States presidential election"), conservative Republican [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley") defeated silverite [William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan").[[171]](#cite_note-175)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/McKinleyAssassination.jpg/250px-McKinleyAssassination.jpg)](/wiki/File:McKinleyAssassination.jpg)

The [assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_William_McKinley "Assassination of William McKinley") of [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley") by [Leon Czolgosz](/wiki/Leon_Czolgosz "Leon Czolgosz") in [Buffalo, New York](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") on September 6, 1901, depicted in a portrait by T. Dart Walker

Prosperity returned under McKinley. The gold standard was enacted, and the tariff was raised. By 1900, the U.S. had the strongest economy in the world.[[172]](#cite_note-Morgan-1966-176) McKinley was [assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_William_McKinley "Assassination of William McKinley") by [Leon Czolgosz](/wiki/Leon_Czolgosz "Leon Czolgosz") in 1901, and was succeeded by [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt").[[173]](#cite_note-177)

The period also saw a major transformation of the banking system, with the arrival of the first [credit union](/wiki/Credit_union "Credit union") in 1908 and the creation of the [Federal Reserve System](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_System "Federal Reserve System") in 1913.[[174]](#cite_note-178)[[175]](#cite_note-179) Apart from two short recessions in [1907](/wiki/Panic_of_1907 "Panic of 1907") and [1920](/wiki/Depression_of_1920%E2%80%931921 "Depression of 1920–1921"), the economy remained prosperous and growing until 1929.[[172]](#cite_note-Morgan-1966-176)

#### Imperialism

Further information: [American imperialism](/wiki/American_imperialism "American imperialism") and [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Victor_Gillam_A_Thing_Well_Begun_Is_Half_Done_1899_Cornell_CUL_PJM_1136_01.jpg/250px-Victor_Gillam_A_Thing_Well_Begun_Is_Half_Done_1899_Cornell_CUL_PJM_1136_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victor_Gillam_A_Thing_Well_Begun_Is_Half_Done_1899_Cornell_CUL_PJM_1136_01.jpg)

A cartoon reflecting [*Judge* magazine](/wiki/Judge_(magazine) "Judge (magazine)") view that the U.S. maintained imperial ambitions following its quick victory in the [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War") in 1898[[176]](#cite_note-180)

The [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") continued to fight [wars with Native Americans](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars") as settlers encroached on their traditional lands. Gradually the U.S. purchased tribal lands and extinguished their claims, forcing most tribes onto subsidized [reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation"). According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau") in 1894, from 1789 to 1894, the Indian Wars killed 19,000 white people and more than 30,000 Indians.[[177]](#cite_note-181)

The [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War") began when Spain refused American demands to reform its oppressive policies in [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba").[[178]](#cite_note-182) The war was a series of quick American victories on land and at sea. At the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1898) "Treaty of Paris (1898)") peace conference the United States acquired the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), and [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam").[[179]](#cite_note-Harrington-183) Cuba became an independent country, under close American tutelage. [William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan") led his Democratic Party in opposition to control of the Philippines, which he denounced as [imperialism](/wiki/American_imperialism "American imperialism").[[179]](#cite_note-Harrington-183) After defeating an [insurrection by Filipino nationalists](/wiki/Philippine%E2%80%93American_War "Philippine–American War"), the United States achieved little in the Philippines except in education. Infrastructural development lost much of its early vigor with the failure of the railroads.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStanley269–272-184)

By 1908, however, Americans lost interest in an empire and turned their international attention to the Caribbean, especially the building of the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal"). The canal opened in 1914 and increased trade with Japan and the rest of the Far East. A key innovation was the [Open Door Policy](/wiki/Open_Door_Policy "Open Door Policy"), whereby the imperial powers were given equal access to Chinese business, with none of them allowed to take control of China.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJensen_et_al.-185)

#### Women's suffrage

Main article: [Women's suffrage in the United States](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_the_United_States "Women's suffrage in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Suffragists_Parade_Down_Fifth_Avenue%2C_1917.JPG/250px-Suffragists_Parade_Down_Fifth_Avenue%2C_1917.JPG)](/wiki/File:Suffragists_Parade_Down_Fifth_Avenue,_1917.JPG)

A [Women's suffragists](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") parade in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") in October 1917, featuring placards with the signatures of more than a million women[[182]](#cite_note-186)

The women's suffrage movement reorganized after the Civil War. By the end of the 19th century, a few Western states had granted women full voting rights,[[108]](#cite_note-Mead-2006-111) and women gained rights in areas such as property and child custody law.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiley2001?-187)

Around 1912, the [feminist movement](/wiki/Feminist_movement "Feminist movement") reawakened, putting an emphasis on its demands for equality and arguing that the corruption of American politics demanded purification by women.[[184]](#cite_note-188) [Alice Paul](/wiki/Alice_Paul "Alice Paul") split from the large, moderate [National American Woman Suffrage Association](/wiki/National_American_Woman_Suffrage_Association "National American Woman Suffrage Association") (NAWSA), led by [Carrie Chapman Catt](/wiki/Carrie_Chapman_Catt "Carrie Chapman Catt"), and formed the more militant [National Woman's Party](/wiki/National_Woman%27s_Party "National Woman's Party"). Suffragists were arrested during their "[Silent Sentinels](/wiki/Silent_Sentinels "Silent Sentinels")" pickets at the White House and taken as [political prisoners](/wiki/Political_prisoner "Political prisoner").[[185]](#cite_note-189)

The anti-suffragist argument that only men could fight in a war, therefore only men deserved the right to vote, was refuted by the participation of American women on the [home front in World War I](/wiki/United_States_home_front_during_World_War_I "United States home front during World War I"). The success of women's suffrage was demonstrated by the politics of some U.S. states that were already allowing women to vote, including Montana, which elected the first woman to the House of Representatives, [Jeannette Rankin](/wiki/Jeannette_Rankin "Jeannette Rankin"). The main resistance came from the South, where white leaders were worried about the threat of black women being allowed to vote. Congress passed the [Nineteenth Amendment](/wiki/Nineteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") in 1919, and women first voted in 1920.[[186]](#cite_note-190) Politicians responded to the new electorate by emphasizing issues of special interest to women, especially [prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States"), child health, and world peace.[[187]](#cite_note-191)[[188]](#cite_note-192)

## Modern America and World Wars (1914–1945)

### World War I and the interwar years

Main article: [History of the United States (1917–1945)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1917%E2%80%931945) "History of the United States (1917–1945)")

See also: [American entry into World War I](/wiki/American_entry_into_World_War_I "American entry into World War I"), [United States home front during World War I](/wiki/United_States_home_front_during_World_War_I "United States home front during World War I"), [United States in World War I](/wiki/United_States_in_World_War_I "United States in World War I"), and [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Cimeti%C3%A8re_am%C3%A9ricain_de_Romagne-sous-Montfaucon_-_1918_-_France.JPG/250px-Cimeti%C3%A8re_am%C3%A9ricain_de_Romagne-sous-Montfaucon_-_1918_-_France.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cimeti%C3%A8re_am%C3%A9ricain_de_Romagne-sous-Montfaucon_-_1918_-_France.JPG)

[Meuse-Argonne American Cemetery](/wiki/Meuse-Argonne_American_Cemetery "Meuse-Argonne American Cemetery") in [France](/wiki/France "France"), a cemetery for [U.S. servicemen](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") killed in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") during their service in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I")

As [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") raged in Europe from 1914, President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") declared neutrality, but warned Germany that resumption of [unrestricted submarine warfare](/wiki/Unrestricted_submarine_warfare "Unrestricted submarine warfare") against American ships would mean war. Germany decided to take the risk, and try to win by cutting off supplies to Britain through the sinking of ships such as the [RMS *Lusitania*](/wiki/Sinking_of_the_RMS_Lusitania "Sinking of the RMS Lusitania"). The U.S. [declared war](/wiki/United_States_declaration_of_war_on_Germany_(1917) "United States declaration of war on Germany (1917)") in April 1917.[[189]](#cite_note-193)

By the summer of 1918 soldiers in General [John J. Pershing](/wiki/John_J._Pershing "John J. Pershing")'s [American Expeditionary Forces](/wiki/American_Expeditionary_Forces "American Expeditionary Forces") arrived at the rate of 10,000 a day, while Germany was unable to replace its losses.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoffman-194) Dissent against the war was suppressed by the [Sedition Act of 1918](/wiki/Sedition_Act_of_1918 "Sedition Act of 1918") and [Espionage Act of 1917](/wiki/Espionage_Act_of_1917 "Espionage Act of 1917"). Over 2,000 were imprisoned for speaking out against the war.[[191]](#cite_note-Kohn-1996-195)

The [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") won in November 1918. Wilson dominated the [1919 Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/1919_Paris_Peace_Conference "1919 Paris Peace Conference"), putting his geopolitical hopes in the new [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") as Germany was treated harshly in the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") (1919). Wilson refused to compromise with Senate Republicans over the issue of Congressional power to declare war, and the Senate rejected the Treaty and the League.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECooper-196) Instead, the United States chose to pursue [unilateralism](/wiki/Unilateralism "Unilateralism").[[193]](#cite_note-197) The aftershock of Russia's [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") resulted in fears of Communism in the United States, leading to a [Red Scare](/wiki/First_Red_Scare "First Red Scare") and the deportation of non-citizens considered subversive.

Despite the Progressive-era modernization of hospitals and medical schools,[[194]](#cite_note-198) the country lost around 550,000 lives to the [Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu") pandemic in 1918 and 1919.[[195]](#cite_note-199)[[196]](#cite_note-200) During the "[Roaring](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties")" 1920s, the economy expanded. African-Americans benefited from the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") and had more cultural power, an example being the [Harlem Renaissance](/wiki/Harlem_Renaissance "Harlem Renaissance") which spread [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") music. Meanwhile, the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") had a resurgence, and the [Immigration Act of 1924](/wiki/Immigration_Act_of_1924 "Immigration Act of 1924") was passed to strictly limit the number of new entries.[[197]](#cite_note-201)

[Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") began in 1920, when the manufacture, sale, import and export of alcohol were prohibited by the [Eighteenth Amendment](/wiki/Eighteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Eighteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution"). [Bootlegged alcohol](/wiki/Rum-running "Rum-running") in the cities ended up under the control of gangs, who fought each other for territory. Italian bootleggers in New York City gradually formed the [Mafia](/wiki/American_Mafia "American Mafia") crime syndicate. In 1933, President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") [repealed prohibition](/wiki/Cullen-Harrison_Act "Cullen-Harrison Act").[[198]](#cite_note-202)

### Great Depression and the New Deal

Main articles: [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal"), [Presidency of Herbert Hoover](/wiki/Presidency_of_Herbert_Hoover "Presidency of Herbert Hoover"), and [Presidency of Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Presidency_of_Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Presidency of Franklin D. Roosevelt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Money_supply_during_the_great_depression_era.png/250px-Money_supply_during_the_great_depression_era.png)](/wiki/File:Money_supply_during_the_great_depression_era.png)

A depiction of the sharp decrease of the money supply between [Black Tuesday](/wiki/Black_Tuesday "Black Tuesday") and the [Bank Holiday](/wiki/Emergency_Banking_Act_of_1933 "Emergency Banking Act of 1933") when massive [bank runs](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") commence across the United States in March 1933

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_States "Great Depression in the United States") (1929–1939) and the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") (1933–1936) were decisive moments in American political, economic, and social history.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy,_Freedom_from_Fear-203) A [financial bubble](/wiki/Financial_bubble "Financial bubble") was fueled by an inflated stock market, which led to the [Wall Street crash](/wiki/Wall_Street_crash "Wall Street crash") on October 29, 1929.[[200]](#cite_note-204) This, along with [other economic factors](/wiki/Causes_of_the_Great_Depression "Causes of the Great Depression"), triggered a worldwide [depression](/wiki/Economic_depression "Economic depression"). The United States experienced [deflation](/wiki/Deflation "Deflation") as prices fell, unemployment soared from 3% in 1929 to 25% in 1933, farm prices fell by half, and manufacturing output plunged by one-third.

The New Deal enacted by President Franklin D. Roosevelt was a series of permanent reform programs including [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)"), [unemployment relief and insurance](/wiki/Social_Security_Act "Social Security Act"), [public housing](/wiki/National_Housing_Act_of_1934 "National Housing Act of 1934"), [bankruptcy insurance](/wiki/1933_Banking_Act "1933 Banking Act"), [farm subsidies](/wiki/Agricultural_Adjustment_Act "Agricultural Adjustment Act"), and [regulation of financial securities](/wiki/Securities_Act_of_1933 "Securities Act of 1933").[[201]](#cite_note-Kennedy-2009-205) It also provided unemployment relief through the [Works Progress Administration](/wiki/Works_Progress_Administration "Works Progress Administration") (WPA) and for young men, the [Civilian Conservation Corps](/wiki/Civilian_Conservation_Corps "Civilian Conservation Corps"). Large-scale spending projects designed to rebuild infrastructure were under the purview of the [Public Works Administration](/wiki/Public_Works_Administration "Public Works Administration").[[201]](#cite_note-Kennedy-2009-205)

State governments introduced the sales tax to pay for new programs. Ideologically, the New Deal established [modern liberalism in the United States](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States").[[201]](#cite_note-Kennedy-2009-205) The [New Deal coalition](/wiki/New_Deal_coalition "New Deal coalition") won re-election for Roosevelt in [1936](/wiki/1936_United_States_presidential_election "1936 United States presidential election"), [1940](/wiki/1940_United_States_presidential_election "1940 United States presidential election"), and [1944](/wiki/1944_United_States_presidential_election "1944 United States presidential election").[[201]](#cite_note-Kennedy-2009-205) The [Second New Deal](/wiki/Second_New_Deal "Second New Deal") in 1935 and 1936 brought the economy further left, building up labor unions through the [Wagner Act](/wiki/Wagner_Act "Wagner Act"). Roosevelt weakened his second term by a failed effort to pack the Supreme Court, which had been a center of conservative resistance to his programs.[[201]](#cite_note-Kennedy-2009-205) The economy essentially recovered by 1936, but long-term unemployment remained a problem until it was solved by wartime spending. Most of the relief programs were dropped in the 1940s, when the conservatives regained power in Congress through the [Conservative coalition](/wiki/Conservative_coalition "Conservative coalition").[[201]](#cite_note-Kennedy-2009-205)

### World War II

Main articles: [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), [United States in World War II](/wiki/United_States_in_World_War_II "United States in World War II"), and [Military history of the United States during World War II](/wiki/Military_history_of_the_United_States_during_World_War_II "Military history of the United States during World War II")

See also: [Historiography of World War II](/wiki/Historiography_of_World_War_II "Historiography of World War II") and [United States home front during World War II](/wiki/United_States_home_front_during_World_War_II "United States home front during World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/The_USS_Arizona_%28BB-39%29_burning_after_the_Japanese_attack_on_Pearl_Harbor_-_NARA_195617_-_Edit.jpg/250px-The_USS_Arizona_%28BB-39%29_burning_after_the_Japanese_attack_on_Pearl_Harbor_-_NARA_195617_-_Edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_USS_Arizona_(BB-39)_burning_after_the_Japanese_attack_on_Pearl_Harbor_-_NARA_195617_-_Edit.jpg)

The [USS *Arizona*](/wiki/USS_Arizona "USS Arizona") burning after the Japanese [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor") on December 7, 1941

During the Depression, the United States remained focused on domestic concerns. U.S. legislation in the [Neutrality Acts](/wiki/Neutrality_Acts_of_the_1930s "Neutrality Acts of the 1930s") sought to avoid foreign conflicts; however, policy clashed with increasing anti-[Nazi](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi") feelings following the German [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") in September 1939 that started [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[202]](#cite_note-Black-2003-206) At first, Roosevelt positioned the U.S. as the "[Arsenal of Democracy](/wiki/Arsenal_of_Democracy "Arsenal of Democracy")", pledging full-scale financial and munitions support for the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") and [Lend-Lease](/wiki/Lend-Lease "Lend-Lease") agreements – but no military personnel.[[202]](#cite_note-Black-2003-206)

Japan tried to neutralize America's power in the Pacific by [attacking Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor") on December 7, 1941, but instead it catalyzed American support to enter the war.[[203]](#cite_note-207) The attack ended a 20-year era of American isolationism which began after the end of WW1 and remains, as of 2026, the last period of isolationism for America.[[204]](#cite_note-208) Roosevelt's [Executive Order 9066](/wiki/Executive_Order_9066 "Executive Order 9066") resulted in over 120,000 Americans of Japanese descent being [removed from their homes and placed in internment camps](/wiki/Internment_of_Japanese_Americans "Internment of Japanese Americans").[[205]](#cite_note-209)[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOgawa_and_Fox135-210)[[207]](#cite_note-Richey2007-211) The Allies fought against Germany in the [European theater](/wiki/European_theater "European theater") and Japan in the [Pacific War](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War").[[208]](#cite_note-EB-1939-212) The United States was one of the "[Allied Big Four](/wiki/Four_Policemen "Four Policemen")", alongside the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), and [China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)").[[209]](#cite_note-213)[[210]](#cite_note-214)

The U.S. gave the Allied war effort money, food, [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), technology, and military personnel. The U.S. focused on maximizing its national economic output, causing a dramatic increase in GDP, the end of unemployment, and a rise in civilian consumption, even as 40% of the GDP went to the war effort.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy,_Freedom_from_Fear-203) A [wartime production boom](/wiki/Military_production_during_World_War_II "Military production during World War II") led to the end of the Great Depression. Tens of millions of workers moved into the active labor force and to higher-productivity jobs. Labor shortages encouraged industries to look for new sources of workers, finding new roles for women and Black people. Economic mobilization was managed by the [War Production Board](/wiki/War_Production_Board "War Production Board").[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy,_Freedom_from_Fear-203) Most durable goods became unavailable or were tightly rationed, while housing for industrial jobs was in short supply. Prices and wages were controlled, and Americans saved a high portion of their incomes, which led to post-war growth.[[211]](#cite_note-215)[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy,_Freedom_from_Fear615–668-216)

The U.S. stopped Japanese expansion in the Pacific in 1942; after the loss of the [Philippines to Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_the_Philippines "Japanese occupation of the Philippines") conquests, as well as a draw in the [Battle of the Coral Sea](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Coral_Sea "Battle of the Coral Sea") in May, the American Navy then inflicted a decisive blow at [Midway](/wiki/Battle_of_Midway "Battle of Midway") in June 1942. The Allied forces built up a garrison on [Guadalcanal](/wiki/Guadalcanal "Guadalcanal") island, formerly held by the Japanese, after the successes of the [Battle of the Eastern Solomons](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Eastern_Solomons "Battle of the Eastern Solomons") and the [Battle of Guadalcanal](/wiki/Battle_of_Guadalcanal "Battle of Guadalcanal"). The Japanese then stopped advancing south, and the U.S. began taking [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea"). Japan also lost [their invasion](/wiki/Aleutian_Islands_campaign "Aleutian Islands campaign") of the Alaskan [Aleutian Islands](/wiki/Aleutian_Islands "Aleutian Islands"), allowing the U.S. to begin attacking the Japanese-controlled [Kuril Islands](/wiki/Kuril_Islands "Kuril Islands").[[208]](#cite_note-EB-1939-212)

American ground forces assisted in the [North African campaign](/wiki/North_African_campaign "North African campaign") and the [collapse of Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy") in 1943. A more significant European front was opened on [D-Day](/wiki/D-Day "D-Day"), June 6, 1944, in which Allied forces invaded [Nazi-occupied France](/wiki/Nazi-occupied_France "Nazi-occupied France").[[208]](#cite_note-EB-1939-212) The Allies began pushing the Germans out of France in the [Normandy campaign](/wiki/Normandy_campaign "Normandy campaign"). After Allied forces landed at the [French Riviera](/wiki/French_Riviera "French Riviera") in [Operation Dragoon](/wiki/Operation_Dragoon "Operation Dragoon"), Hitler allowed his army to retreat from Normandy.[[213]](#cite_note-217) Roosevelt [died](/wiki/Death_of_Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Death of Franklin D. Roosevelt") in 1945, and was succeeded by [Harry Truman](/wiki/Harry_Truman "Harry Truman").[[214]](#cite_note-218) The western front stopped short of Berlin, leaving the Soviets to take it in the [Battle of Berlin](/wiki/Battle_of_Berlin "Battle of Berlin"). The Nazi regime [formally capitulated](/wiki/German_Instrument_of_Surrender "German Instrument of Surrender") in May 1945, [ending the war in Europe](/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Europe "End of World War II in Europe").[[208]](#cite_note-EB-1939-212)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Raising_the_Flag_on_Iwo_Jima%2C_larger_-_edit1.jpg/250px-Raising_the_Flag_on_Iwo_Jima%2C_larger_-_edit1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Raising_the_Flag_on_Iwo_Jima,_larger_-_edit1.jpg)

*[Raising the Flag on Iwo Jima](/wiki/Raising_the_Flag_on_Iwo_Jima "Raising the Flag on Iwo Jima")*, a photo of [U.S. Marines](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") raising a U.S. flag atop [Mount Suribachi](/wiki/Mount_Suribachi "Mount Suribachi") during the [Battle of Iwo Jima](/wiki/Battle_of_Iwo_Jima "Battle of Iwo Jima") on February 23, 1945

In the Pacific, the U.S. implemented an [island hopping strategy](/wiki/Leapfrogging_(strategy) "Leapfrogging (strategy)") toward Tokyo. The Philippines was eventually reconquered, after Japan and the United States fought in history's largest naval battle, the [Battle of Leyte Gulf](/wiki/Battle_of_Leyte_Gulf "Battle of Leyte Gulf").[[215]](#cite_note-219) After the war, the U.S. [granted the Philippines independence](/wiki/Treaty_of_Manila_(1946) "Treaty of Manila (1946)").[[216]](#cite_note-Gov.Ph-220)

Military [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") increased during the war, leading to the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project"), a secret effort to harness [nuclear fission](/wiki/Nuclear_fission "Nuclear fission") to produce [atomic bombs](/wiki/Atomic_bombs "Atomic bombs");[[217]](#cite_note-221) the first nuclear device was [detonated on July 16, 1945](/wiki/Trinity_(nuclear_test) "Trinity (nuclear test)").[[218]](#cite_note-222) U.S. airfields in the [Mariana Islands](/wiki/Mariana_Islands "Mariana Islands") allowed for easier bombing of Japan and hard-fought U.S. victories at [Iwo Jima](/wiki/Battle_of_Iwo_Jima "Battle of Iwo Jima") and [Okinawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Okinawa "Battle of Okinawa") in 1945.[[219]](#cite_note-223) The U.S. initially prepared to [invade Japan's home islands](/wiki/Operation_Downfall "Operation Downfall"), but they [dropped atomic bombs](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki") on the Japanese cities of [Hiroshima](/wiki/Hiroshima "Hiroshima") and [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki") in August 1945, killing at least 200,000 residents in both cities, compelling Japan to surrender and ending World War II.[[220]](#cite_note-224) The U.S. [occupied Japan](/wiki/Occupied_Japan "Occupied Japan") (and [part of Germany](/wiki/American_occupation_zone_in_Germany "American occupation zone in Germany")).[[221]](#cite_note-225) Throughout the 4 years of American involvement in World War II, 400,000 American military personnel and civilians were killed.[[222]](#cite_note-226) Nuclear weapons have not been used since the war ended, and a "[long peace](/wiki/Long_Peace "Long Peace")" began between the global powers, but they still competed in the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War").[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis,_Long_Peace-227)

## Cold War (1945–1991)

Main articles: [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), [History of the United States (1945–1964)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1945%E2%80%931964) "History of the United States (1945–1964)"), [History of the United States (1964–1980)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1964%E2%80%931980) "History of the United States (1964–1980)"), and [History of the United States (1980–1991)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1980%E2%80%931991) "History of the United States (1980–1991)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/NATO_vs._Warsaw_Pact_%281949-1990%29.svg/250px-NATO_vs._Warsaw_Pact_%281949-1990%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NATO_vs._Warsaw_Pact_(1949-1990).svg)

The [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") (blue) and [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact") (red) alliances during the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") from 1949 to 1990

### Economic boom and the beginning of the Cold War

#### Truman administration

Main articles: [Presidency of Harry S. Truman](/wiki/Presidency_of_Harry_S._Truman "Presidency of Harry S. Truman") and [Cold War (1948–1953)](/wiki/Cold_War_(1948%E2%80%931953) "Cold War (1948–1953)")

In the decades after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the United States became a global influence in economic, political, military, cultural, and technological affairs. Following World War II, the United States emerged as one of the two dominant superpowers, the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") being the other. The U.S. Senate approved U.S. participation in the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN), which marked a turn away from traditional [American isolationism](/wiki/American_isolationism "American isolationism") and toward increased international involvement.[[224]](#cite_note-228) The United States and other major Allied powers became the foundation of the [UN Security Council](/wiki/UN_Security_Council "UN Security Council").[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis,_Origins_of_the_Cold_War-229) The [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") (CIA) was created in 1947.[[226]](#cite_note-230)

[![Marshall Plan poster](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Marshall_Plan_poster.JPG/250px-Marshall_Plan_poster.JPG)](/wiki/File:Marshall_Plan_poster.JPG)

One of a number of posters created by the [Economic Cooperation Administration](/wiki/Economic_Cooperation_Administration "Economic Cooperation Administration"), an agency of the [U.S. government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"), to sell the [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan") in Europe following the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")

The U.S. wished to rescue Europe from the devastation of World War II, and to contain the expansion of [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"), represented by the Soviet Union. U.S. foreign policy during the Cold War was built around the support of Western Europe and Japan along with the policy of [containment](/wiki/Containment "Containment") (containing the spread of communism).[[227]](#cite_note-Blakeley-2009-231) The [Truman Doctrine](/wiki/Truman_Doctrine "Truman Doctrine") in 1947 was the U.S.' attempt to secure trading partners in Europe, by providing military and economic aid to Greece and Turkey to counteract the threat of communist expansion in the Balkans.[[228]](#cite_note-232)[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis,_Long_Peace-227) In 1948, the United States replaced piecemeal financial aid programs with a comprehensive [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan"), which pumped money into Western Europe, and removed trade barriers, while modernizing the managerial practices of businesses and governments.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis,_Cold_War-233) Post-war American aid to Europe totaled $25 billion, out of the U.S. GDP of $258 billion in 1948.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis,_Cold_War-233)

In 1949, the United States, rejecting the long-standing policy of no military alliances in peacetime, formed the [North Atlantic Treaty Organization](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Treaty_Organization "North Atlantic Treaty Organization") (NATO) alliance. In response, the Soviets formed the [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact") of communist states, leading to the "[Iron Curtain](/wiki/Iron_Curtain "Iron Curtain")".[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis,_Cold_War-233) In 1949, the Soviets performed their [first nuclear weapon test](/wiki/RDS-1 "RDS-1").[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis,_Long_Peace-227) This escalated the risk of nuclear warfare; the threat of [mutually assured destruction](/wiki/Mutually_assured_destruction "Mutually assured destruction"), however, prevented both powers from nuclear war, and resulted the proxy wars in which the two sides did not directly confront each other.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis,_Long_Peace-227)
