The U.S. fought against communists in the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") and [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), and [toppled left-wing governments in the third world](/wiki/United_States_involvement_in_regime_change#1945–1991:_Cold_War "United States involvement in regime change") to try to stop its spread, such as [Iran in 1953](/wiki/1953_Iranian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1953 Iranian coup d'état") and [Guatemala in 1954](/wiki/1954_Guatemalan_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1954 Guatemalan coup d'état").[[227]](#cite_note-Blakeley-2009-231) [McCarthyism](/wiki/McCarthyism "McCarthyism") was a widespread government program led by Senator [Joseph McCarthy](/wiki/Joseph_McCarthy "Joseph McCarthy") to expose communists in government and business. Hollywood was targeted by the [House Un-American Activities Committee](/wiki/House_Un-American_Activities_Committee "House Un-American Activities Committee").[[230]](#cite_note-234) Gay people were targeted under the McCarthyist [Lavender Scare](/wiki/Lavender_Scare "Lavender Scare").[[231]](#cite_note-235)

#### Eisenhower administration

Main articles: [Presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Presidency_of_Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower") and [Cold War (1953–1962)](/wiki/Cold_War_(1953%E2%80%931962) "Cold War (1953–1962)")

[Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower") was [elected president](/wiki/1952_United_States_presidential_election "1952 United States presidential election") in 1952 in a landslide.[[232]](#cite_note-236) He ended the Korean War, and avoided any other major conflict. He cut military spending by relying on advanced technology, such as nuclear weapons carried by [long-range bombers](/wiki/Long-range_bomber "Long-range bomber") and [intercontinental missiles](/wiki/Intercontinental_missile "Intercontinental missile").[[233]](#cite_note-Hitchcock-2018-237) After [Stalin died in 1953](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Joseph_Stalin "Death and state funeral of Joseph Stalin"), Eisenhower worked to obtain friendlier relationships with the Soviet Union. At home, he ended McCarthyism, expanded the Social Security program, and presided over a decade of bipartisan cooperation.[[233]](#cite_note-Hitchcock-2018-237)

Domestically, after 1948, America entered an [economic boom](/wiki/Post%E2%80%93World_War_II_economic_expansion "Post–World War II economic expansion"): 60% of the American population had attained a "middle-class" standard of living by the mid-1950s, compared with only 31% in the 1928 and 1929. Between 1947 and 1960, the average real income for American workers increased by as much as it had in the previous half-century.[[234]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-238) The economy allowed for an affordable lifestyle with large families; this created the [baby boom](/wiki/Baby_boom "Baby boom"), in which millions of children were born at increased rates from 1945 to 1964.[[235]](#cite_note-239) Many Americans [moved into suburban neighborhoods](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization").[[236]](#cite_note-240)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Operation_Arkansas%2C_Little_Rock_Nine.jpg/250px-Operation_Arkansas%2C_Little_Rock_Nine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Operation_Arkansas,_Little_Rock_Nine.jpg)

The [101st Airborne Division](/wiki/101st_Airborne_Division "101st Airborne Division") escorting the [Little Rock Nine](/wiki/Little_Rock_Nine "Little Rock Nine") into [Little Rock Central High School](/wiki/Little_Rock_Central_High_School "Little Rock Central High School") in September 1957

In 1954, the Supreme Court ruled on *[Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education")*, finding public school segregation unconstitutional.[[237]](#cite_note-EB-2024b-241) When [nine Black students](/wiki/Little_Rock_Nine "Little Rock Nine") were threatened over their admission into all-white [Little Rock Central High School](/wiki/Little_Rock_Central_High_School "Little Rock Central High School"), Eisenhower sent in a thousand National Guard troops to ensure peace.[[233]](#cite_note-Hitchcock-2018-237) Starting in the late 1950s, institutionalized [racism across the United States](/wiki/Racism_in_the_United_States "Racism in the United States"), but especially in the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), was increasingly challenged by the growing [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"). The activism of [Rosa Parks](/wiki/Rosa_Parks "Rosa Parks") and [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") led to the [boycott of segregated public buses](/wiki/Montgomery_bus_boycott "Montgomery bus boycott") in [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery,_Alabama "Montgomery, Alabama"), Alabama in 1955, organized by King and the [Montgomery Improvement Association](/wiki/Montgomery_Improvement_Association "Montgomery Improvement Association"). They faced multiple acts of violence, but continued the boycott for a year, until the Supreme Court ordered the city to desegregate the buses.[[237]](#cite_note-EB-2024b-241)

The Soviets unexpectedly surpassed American technology in 1957 with [Sputnik](/wiki/Sputnik "Sputnik"), the first Earth satellite. The [R-7 missile](/wiki/R-7_Semyorka "R-7 Semyorka") which launched Sputnik into space could have hypothetically dropped a nuclear bomb into U.S. air space [from above](/wiki/High-altitude_nuclear_explosion "High-altitude nuclear explosion"); new American fears regarding Soviet power began the [Space Race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race"), a competition between the two countries to prove their technological superiority through space exploration. In 1958, Eisenhower created the [National Aeronautics and Space Administration](/wiki/National_Aeronautics_and_Space_Administration "National Aeronautics and Space Administration") (NASA) for this purpose. Angst about the weaknesses of American education led to large-scale federal support for [science education](/wiki/Science_education#United_States "Science education") and research.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson,_Grand_Expectations-242)

### Civil unrest and social reforms

Main articles: [History of the United States (1964–1980)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1964%E2%80%931980) "History of the United States (1964–1980)") and [Cold War (1962–1979)](/wiki/Cold_War_(1962%E2%80%931979) "Cold War (1962–1979)")

Further information: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy "Presidency of John F. Kennedy"), [Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson"), and [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society")

In 1960, [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") was [elected President](/wiki/1960_United_States_presidential_election "1960 United States presidential election"). [His administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy "Presidency of John F. Kennedy") saw the acceleration of the country's role in the Space Race, escalation of the American role in the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), the [Bay of Pigs Invasion](/wiki/Bay_of_Pigs_Invasion "Bay of Pigs Invasion"), and the [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis"). President Kennedy [was assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy") on November 22, 1963.[[239]](#cite_note-243) [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") then became president.[[240]](#cite_note-Schneider-2009-244) He secured congressional passage of his [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society") programs,[[241]](#cite_note-245) dealing with civil rights, the end of legal segregation, [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)"), extension of welfare, federal aid to education at all levels, subsidies for the arts and humanities, [environmental activism](/wiki/Environmental_movement_in_the_United_States "Environmental movement in the United States"), and a [series of programs designed to wipe out poverty](/wiki/War_on_Poverty "War on Poverty").[[242]](#cite_note-246)[[243]](#cite_note-247)

#### Civil rights and counterculture movements

Main articles: [Civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"), [Counterculture of the 1960s](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s"), and [Second-wave feminism](/wiki/Second-wave_feminism "Second-wave feminism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Civil_Rights_March_on_Washington%2C_D.C._%28Leaders_marching_from_the_Washington_Monument_to_the_Lincoln_Memorial%29_-_NARA_-_542010.jpg/250px-Civil_Rights_March_on_Washington%2C_D.C._%28Leaders_marching_from_the_Washington_Monument_to_the_Lincoln_Memorial%29_-_NARA_-_542010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Civil_Rights_March_on_Washington,_D.C._(Leaders_marching_from_the_Washington_Monument_to_the_Lincoln_Memorial)_-_NARA_-_542010.jpg)

Civil rights activists during the [March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom](/wiki/March_on_Washington_for_Jobs_and_Freedom "March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") in August 1963

For years, [nonviolent](/wiki/Nonviolent "Nonviolent") civil rights activists organized direct actions, such as the 1963 [Birmingham campaign](/wiki/Birmingham_campaign "Birmingham campaign"), the 1965 [Selma to Montgomery march](/wiki/Selma_to_Montgomery_march "Selma to Montgomery march"), and the 1966 [Chicago open housing movement](/wiki/Chicago_open_housing_movement "Chicago open housing movement"), where they also became victims of violence. Along with Supreme Court decisions like *[Loving v. Virginia](/wiki/Loving_v._Virginia "Loving v. Virginia")* and the 1963 [March on Washington](/wiki/March_on_Washington "March on Washington"), these movements achieved great steps toward equality with laws like the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"), the [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), and the [Fair Housing Act of 1968](/wiki/Fair_Housing_Act_of_1968 "Fair Housing Act of 1968"). These ended the [Jim Crow laws](/wiki/Jim_Crow_laws "Jim Crow laws") that had legalized [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation").[[244]](#cite_note-248) Native Americans protested federal courts, highlighting the federal government's failure to honor treaties involving them. One of the most outspoken Native American groups was the [American Indian Movement](/wiki/American_Indian_Movement "American Indian Movement") (AIM). In the 1960s, [Cesar Chavez](/wiki/Cesar_Chavez "Cesar Chavez") began organizing poorly paid [Mexican-American](/wiki/Mexican-American "Mexican-American") farm workers in California, eventually forming the country's first successful union of farm workers, the [United Farm Workers of America](/wiki/United_Farm_Workers_of_America "United Farm Workers of America") (UFW).[[245]](#cite_note-249)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Vietnam._As_the_second_phase_of_operation_%22Thayer%2C%22_the_1st_Air_Cavalry_Division_%28airmobile%29_is_having..._-_NARA_-_530612.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Vietnam._As_the_second_phase_of_operation_%22Thayer%2C%22_the_1st_Air_Cavalry_Division_%28airmobile%29_is_having..._-_NARA_-_530612.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vietnam._As_the_second_phase_of_operation_%22Thayer,%22_the_1st_Air_Cavalry_Division_(airmobile)_is_having..._-_NARA_-_530612.tif)

U.S. soldiers searching a village for potential [Viet Cong](/wiki/Viet_Cong "Viet Cong") during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") in October 1966

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/A_female_demonstrator_offers_a_flower_to_military_police_on_guard_at_the_Pentagon_during_an_anti-Vietnam_demonstration._Arlington%2C_Virginia%2C_USA.jpg/250px-A_female_demonstrator_offers_a_flower_to_military_police_on_guard_at_the_Pentagon_during_an_anti-Vietnam_demonstration._Arlington%2C_Virginia%2C_USA.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_female_demonstrator_offers_a_flower_to_military_police_on_guard_at_the_Pentagon_during_an_anti-Vietnam_demonstration._Arlington,_Virginia,_USA.jpg)

An [anti-Vietnam War demonstration](/wiki/Opposition_to_United_States_involvement_in_the_Vietnam_War "Opposition to United States involvement in the Vietnam War") outside [the Pentagon](/wiki/The_Pentagon "The Pentagon") in October 1967

Amid the Cold War, the United States entered the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), whose growing unpopularity fed already existing social movements. [Feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism") and the [environmental movement](/wiki/Environmental_movement "Environmental movement") became political forces, and progress continued toward [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") for all Americans. A [counterculture revolution](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s") in the late sixties and early seventies further divided Americans in a "culture war" but also brought forth more liberated social views.[[246]](#cite_note-250) Frustrations with the seemingly slow progress of the integration movement led to the emergence of more radical politics, such as the [Black Power](/wiki/Black_Power "Black Power") movement.[[247]](#cite_note-251) The summer of 1967 saw opposing philosophies in two widespread movements, the more peaceful [Summer of Love](/wiki/Summer_of_Love "Summer of Love") and the radical [long, hot summer](/wiki/Long,_hot_summer_of_1967 "Long, hot summer of 1967"), which included nationwide riots.[[248]](#cite_note-252) Martin Luther King Jr. was [assassinated in 1968](/wiki/Assassination_of_Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.").[[249]](#cite_note-Lupo-2010-253) The modern [gay rights movement](/wiki/Gay_rights_movement "Gay rights movement") started after the [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots") in 1969.[[250]](#cite_note-254)

A new consciousness of the inequality of American women began sweeping the nation, starting with the 1963 publication of [Betty Friedan](/wiki/Betty_Friedan "Betty Friedan")'s best-seller, *[The Feminine Mystique](/wiki/The_Feminine_Mystique "The Feminine Mystique")*, which critiqued the American cultural idea that women could only find fulfillment through their roles as wives, mothers, and keepers of the home. In 1966, Friedan and others established the [National Organization for Women](/wiki/National_Organization_for_Women "National Organization for Women") (NOW) to advocate for women's rights.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiley2001?-187)[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZophy-255) Protests began, and the new women's liberation movement grew in size and power, gaining much media attention.[[252]](#cite_note-Critchlow-2005-256) The proposed [Equal Rights Amendment](/wiki/Equal_Rights_Amendment "Equal Rights Amendment") to the Constitution, passed by Congress in 1972, was defeated by a conservative coalition mobilized by [Phyllis Schlafly](/wiki/Phyllis_Schlafly "Phyllis Schlafly").[[252]](#cite_note-Critchlow-2005-256)[[253]](#cite_note-Mansbridge-1986-257) However, many federal laws established women's equal status under the law, such as those [equalizing pay](/wiki/Equal_Pay_Act_of_1963 "Equal Pay Act of 1963"), [employment](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"), [education](/wiki/Title_IX "Title IX"), [employment opportunities](/wiki/Equal_Employment_Opportunity_Act_of_1972 "Equal Employment Opportunity Act of 1972"), and [credit](/wiki/Equal_Credit_Opportunity_Act "Equal Credit Opportunity Act") between genders, and [ending pregnancy discrimination](/wiki/Pregnancy_Discrimination_Act "Pregnancy Discrimination Act"). State laws criminalized [spousal abuse](/wiki/Spousal_abuse "Spousal abuse") and [marital rape](/wiki/Marital_rape "Marital rape"), and the Supreme Court ruled that the [equal protection clause](/wiki/Equal_protection_clause "Equal protection clause") of the [Fourteenth Amendment](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") applied to women. Social custom and consciousness began to change, accepting women's equality. Abortion, deemed by the Supreme Court as a [fundamental right](/wiki/Fundamental_right "Fundamental right") in *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade")* (1973), is still a point of debate.

### Détente

Main article: [Détente](/wiki/D%C3%A9tente "Détente")

#### Nixon and Ford administrations

Main articles: [Presidency of Richard Nixon](/wiki/Presidency_of_Richard_Nixon "Presidency of Richard Nixon") and [Presidency of Gerald Ford](/wiki/Presidency_of_Gerald_Ford "Presidency of Gerald Ford")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/A_Man_on_the_Moon%2C_AS11-40-5903_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-A_Man_on_the_Moon%2C_AS11-40-5903_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Man_on_the_Moon,_AS11-40-5903_(cropped).jpg)

[Buzz Aldrin](/wiki/Buzz_Aldrin "Buzz Aldrin") (shown) and [Neil Armstrong](/wiki/Neil_Armstrong "Neil Armstrong") became the first humans to walk on the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") during [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA")'s [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11") mission in July 1969.

President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") (1969–1974) largely continued the New Deal and Great Society programs he inherited.[[240]](#cite_note-Schneider-2009-244)[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohn_Greene-258) Nixon created the [Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency"),[[255]](#cite_note-259) [opened relations](/wiki/1972_visit_by_Richard_Nixon_to_China "1972 visit by Richard Nixon to China") with China, and [attempted to gradually turn the Vietnam War effort over to](/wiki/Vietnamization "Vietnamization") the [South Vietnamese](/wiki/South_Vietnam "South Vietnam"). He negotiated the [peace treaty in 1973](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Accords "Paris Peace Accords") which secured the release of POWs and led to the withdrawal of U.S. troops. The war had cost the lives of 58,000 American troops. Nixon manipulated the fierce distrust between the Soviet Union and China to the advantage of the U.S., achieving *[détente](/wiki/D%C3%A9tente "Détente")* with both parties.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohn_Greene-258) He was also president during the U.S.' [landing on the Moon](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11") in 1969.

The [Watergate scandal](/wiki/Watergate_scandal "Watergate scandal"), involving Nixon's cover-up of his operatives' break-in into the [Democratic National Committee](/wiki/Democratic_National_Committee "Democratic National Committee") headquarters, destroyed his political base and forced his resignation on August 9, 1974.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohn_Greene-258) He was succeeded by Vice President [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford").[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohn_Greene-258)[[256]](#cite_note-260)

The [Fall of Saigon](/wiki/Fall_of_Saigon "Fall of Saigon") on April 30, 1975, ended the Vietnam War.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohn_Greene-258) In Central America, the U.S. government supported right-wing governments against left-wing groups, such as in [El Salvador](/wiki/Salvadoran_civil_war "Salvadoran civil war") and [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemalan_civil_war "Guatemalan civil war"). In South America, they supported [Argentina](/wiki/National_Reorganization_Process "National Reorganization Process") and [Chile](/wiki/Military_dictatorship_of_Chile "Military dictatorship of Chile"), who carried out [Operation Condor](/wiki/Operation_Condor "Operation Condor"), a campaign of assassinations of exiled political opponents by [Southern Cone](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone") governments, created at the behest of Chilean dictator [Augusto Pinochet](/wiki/Augusto_Pinochet "Augusto Pinochet") in 1975.[[257]](#cite_note-261)[[258]](#cite_note-262)[[259]](#cite_note-263)

The [OPEC oil embargo](/wiki/OPEC_oil_embargo "OPEC oil embargo") marked a long-term economic transition: energy prices skyrocketed, and American factories faced serious competition from foreign automobiles, clothing, electronics, and consumer goods. By the late 1970s, the economy suffered an [energy crisis](/wiki/1970s_Energy_Crisis "1970s Energy Crisis"), slow economic growth, high unemployment, very high inflation, and high interest rates ([stagflation](/wiki/Stagflation "Stagflation")). Since economists agreed on [deregulation](/wiki/Deregulation "Deregulation"), many of the New Deal era regulations were ended.[[260]](#cite_note-264) Meanwhile, the first mass-market [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") were being developed in California's [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley").[[261]](#cite_note-265)

#### Carter administration

Main article: [Presidency of Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Presidency_of_Jimmy_Carter "Presidency of Jimmy Carter")

[Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") was elected president in 1976.[[262]](#cite_note-266) Carter brokered the [Camp David Accords](/wiki/Camp_David_Accords "Camp David Accords") between Israel and Egypt. In 1979, Iranian students stormed the U.S. embassy in [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran") and [took 66 Americans hostage](/wiki/Iran_hostage_crisis "Iran hostage crisis"). Carter lost the [1980 election](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election") to the Republican [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan").[[263]](#cite_note-267) On January 20, 1981, minutes after Carter's term ended, the remaining U.S. captives were released.

### End of the Cold War

Main article: [History of the United States (1980–1991)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1980%E2%80%931991) "History of the United States (1980–1991)")

See also: [Cold War (1985-1991)](/wiki/Cold_War_(1985-1991) "Cold War (1985-1991)")

#### Reagan administration

Main article: [Presidency of Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan "Presidency of Ronald Reagan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/1981%E2%80%931989_monthly_unemployment%2C_inflation%2C_and_interest_rates.svg/250px-1981%E2%80%931989_monthly_unemployment%2C_inflation%2C_and_interest_rates.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1981%E2%80%931989_monthly_unemployment,_inflation,_and_interest_rates.svg)

Monthly unemployment, inflation, and interest rates from January 1981 to January 1989

President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan")'s conservative policies produced a major [political realignment](/wiki/Political_realignment "Political realignment") with his [1980](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election") and [1984](/wiki/1984_United_States_presidential_election "1984 United States presidential election") landslide elections.[[264]](#cite_note-Caplow-1994-268)[[265]](#cite_note-BEA-2007-269) Reagan's [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") economic policies (dubbed "[Reaganomics](/wiki/Reaganomics "Reaganomics")") included the implementation of the [Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981](/wiki/Economic_Recovery_Tax_Act_of_1981 "Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981").[[265]](#cite_note-BEA-2007-269)[[266]](#cite_note-270)[[267]](#cite_note-271) Reagan continued to downsize government taxation and regulation;[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz140–141-272) New Deal and Great Society programs were ended.[[240]](#cite_note-Schneider-2009-244) The U.S. experienced a [recession in 1982](/wiki/Early_1980s_recession "Early 1980s recession"), but after inflation decreased, unemployment then decreased, and the economic growth rate increased from 4.5% in 1982 to 7.2% in 1984.[[269]](#cite_note-273)[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz170-274) However, homelessness and economic inequality also rose.[[271]](#cite_note-275)[[272]](#cite_note-276)

The Reagan administration's expansion of the [war on drugs](/wiki/War_on_drugs "War on drugs") led to an [increase in incarceration](/wiki/United_States_incarceration_rate "United States incarceration rate"), particularly among African Americans, with the number of people imprisoned for drug offences rising from 50,000 to 400,000 between 1980 and 1997.[[273]](#cite_note-EB-2024a-277)[[274]](#cite_note-278) Manufacturing industries moving out of inner cities increased poverty in those areas; poverty increased drug dealing and contributed to the [crack epidemic](/wiki/Crack_epidemic "Crack epidemic"), which led to increased crime and incarceration.[[273]](#cite_note-EB-2024a-277)[[275]](#cite_note-279) The government also [reacted slowly](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_and_AIDS "Ronald Reagan and AIDS") to the [AIDS crisis](/wiki/HIV/AIDS_in_the_United_States "HIV/AIDS in the United States"), and banned reliable information on the disease, which led to higher infection rates.[[276]](#cite_note-280)[[277]](#cite_note-281)

Reagan ordered a buildup of the U.S. military, incurring additional budget deficits.[[278]](#cite_note-Zelizer-2010a-282) The 1983 [invasion of Grenada](/wiki/Invasion_of_Grenada "Invasion of Grenada") and 1986 [bombing of Libya](/wiki/1986_United_States_bombing_of_Libya "1986 United States bombing of Libya") were popular in the U.S., though Reagan's backing of the [Contra](/wiki/Contras "Contras") rebels was mired in the controversy over the [Iran–Contra affair](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Contra_affair "Iran–Contra affair").[[279]](#cite_note-283) Reagan also introduced a complicated missile defense system known as the [Strategic Defense Initiative](/wiki/Strategic_Defense_Initiative "Strategic Defense Initiative"). The Soviets reacted harshly because they thought it violated the 1972 [Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty](/wiki/Anti-Ballistic_Missile_Treaty "Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty"), and would give the U.S. a major military advantage, so they stopped negotiating [disarmament](/wiki/Disarmament "Disarmament") deals until the late 1980s.[[278]](#cite_note-Zelizer-2010a-282)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/USAF_F-16A_F-15C_F-15E_Desert_Storm_edit2.jpg/250px-USAF_F-16A_F-15C_F-15E_Desert_Storm_edit2.jpg)](/wiki/File:USAF_F-16A_F-15C_F-15E_Desert_Storm_edit2.jpg)

[U.S. Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") aircraft fly over oil fields destroyed by the retreating [Iraqi Ground Forces](/wiki/Iraqi_Ground_Forces "Iraqi Ground Forces") during the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") in 1991

Reagan met four times with Gorbachev, and their summit conferences led to the signing of the [Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty](/wiki/Intermediate-Range_Nuclear_Forces_Treaty "Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty").

#### George H. W. Bush administration

Main articles: [Presidency of George H. W. Bush](/wiki/Presidency_of_George_H._W._Bush "Presidency of George H. W. Bush") and [Post–Cold War era](/wiki/Post%E2%80%93Cold_War_era "Post–Cold War era")

International affairs drove the George H. W. Bush presidency, which navigated the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") and a new era of [U.S.–Soviet relations](/wiki/Soviet_Union%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Soviet Union–United States relations"). In 1989 Bush directed a [military invasion of Panama to overthrow](/wiki/United_States_invasion_of_Panama "United States invasion of Panama") [Manuel Noriega](/wiki/Manuel_Noriega "Manuel Noriega"). On December 3, 1989, Gorbachev and Bush declared the Cold War over at the [Malta Summit](/wiki/Malta_Summit "Malta Summit"). After the fall of the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Berlin_Wall "Berlin Wall"), Bush successfully pushed for the [reunification of Germany](/wiki/Reunification_of_Germany "Reunification of Germany") in close cooperation with West German Chancellor [Helmut Kohl](/wiki/Helmut_Kohl "Helmut Kohl"), overcoming the reluctance of [Gorbachev](/wiki/Gorbachev "Gorbachev"). The [Soviet Union collapsed](/wiki/Soviet_Union_collapse "Soviet Union collapse") in 1991, leaving the United States as the sole superpower.[[280]](#cite_note-284)

## Contemporary United States (1991–present)

Main articles: [History of the United States (1991–2016)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1991%E2%80%932016) "History of the United States (1991–2016)") and [History of the United States (2016–present)](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(2016%E2%80%93present) "History of the United States (2016–present)")

### George H. W. Bush and Clinton administrations

Main articles: [Presidency of George H. W. Bush](/wiki/Presidency_of_George_H._W._Bush "Presidency of George H. W. Bush"), [Presidency of Bill Clinton](/wiki/Presidency_of_Bill_Clinton "Presidency of Bill Clinton"), [Third Way (United States)](/wiki/Third_Way_(United_States) "Third Way (United States)"), and [Republican Revolution](/wiki/Republican_Revolution "Republican Revolution")

After the collapse of the Soviet Union, the United States continued to intervene in international affairs. [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush")'s administration led an [international coalition](/wiki/Coalition_of_the_Gulf_War "Coalition of the Gulf War") against [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") in the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") after [Iraq invaded](/wiki/Iraqi_invasion_of_Kuwait "Iraqi invasion of Kuwait") neighboring Kuwait in 1990. The war undid the Iraqi [annexation](/wiki/Annexation "Annexation") of Kuwait.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz400–401-285) Under Bush, the U.S. also became involved in wars in [Panama](/wiki/United_States_invasion_of_Panama "United States invasion of Panama"), [Somalia](/wiki/Operation_Gothic_Serpent "Operation Gothic Serpent"), [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnian_War "Bosnian War"), and [Croatia](/wiki/Croatian_War_of_Independence "Croatian War of Independence").[[282]](#cite_note-286)[[283]](#cite_note-287)[[284]](#cite_note-288) In 1992, there were [riots in Los Angeles](/wiki/1992_Los_Angeles_riots "1992 Los Angeles riots") over [police brutality](/wiki/Police_brutality_in_the_United_States "Police brutality in the United States").[[285]](#cite_note-289)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Oklahomacitybombing-DF-ST-98-01356.jpg/250px-Oklahomacitybombing-DF-ST-98-01356.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oklahomacitybombing-DF-ST-98-01356.jpg)

Ruins following the [Oklahoma City bombing](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_bombing "Oklahoma City bombing") in April 1995

[Elected in 1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election"), President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") oversaw economic expansion and passed the [first balanced federal budget](/wiki/Economic_policy_of_the_Bill_Clinton_administration "Economic policy of the Bill Clinton administration") in 30 years.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz400-290) Much of the economic boom was a side effect of the [Digital Revolution](/wiki/Digital_Revolution "Digital Revolution"), and new business opportunities created by the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet").[[287]](#cite_note-291) During the [Clinton administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Bill_Clinton "Presidency of Bill Clinton"), the U.S. was involved in wars in [Haiti](/wiki/Operation_Uphold_Democracy "Operation Uphold Democracy") and [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_War "Kosovo War").[[288]](#cite_note-292)[[289]](#cite_note-293)

[Conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") heavily won the [1994 midterm elections](/wiki/1994_midterm_elections "1994 midterm elections") in a "[Republican Revolution](/wiki/Republican_Revolution "Republican Revolution")", which was built around the [Contract with America](/wiki/Contract_with_America "Contract with America") policy agenda.[[290]](#cite_note-Glass-2007-294)[[291]](#cite_note-295) [Newt Gingrich](/wiki/Newt_Gingrich "Newt Gingrich") was chosen as House Speaker,[[290]](#cite_note-Glass-2007-294) and he would heavily influence the Republican Party to engage in "confrontational" political speech.[[292]](#cite_note-296)[[293]](#cite_note-297) Clinton's leadership after the [Oklahoma City bombing](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_bombing "Oklahoma City bombing") increased his popularity,[[294]](#cite_note-298) and he won in the [1996 presidential elections](/wiki/1996_United_States_presidential_election "1996 United States presidential election").[[295]](#cite_note-299) In 1998, [Clinton was impeached](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Bill_Clinton "Impeachment of Bill Clinton") by the House of Representatives on charges of [lying under oath](/wiki/Lying_under_oath "Lying under oath") about a sexual relationship with White House intern [Monica Lewinsky](/wiki/Monica_Lewinsky "Monica Lewinsky"). He was acquitted by the Senate.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz400-290)

In 2000, the [dot-com bubble](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble"), a widespread [overvaluation](/wiki/Overvaluation "Overvaluation") of Internet company stocks, burst and hurt the U.S. economy.[[296]](#cite_note-300)[[297]](#cite_note-301) The [presidential election in 2000](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election "2000 United States presidential election") between Governor [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") was extremely close and produced a dramatic [dispute over the counting of votes](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election_recount_in_Florida "2000 United States presidential election recount in Florida").[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz420–427-302) Bush ultimately won.[[299]](#cite_note-303)

### George W. Bush administration

Main articles: [Presidency of George W. Bush](/wiki/Presidency_of_George_W._Bush "Presidency of George W. Bush"), [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), and [War on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/National_Park_Service_9-11_Statue_of_Liberty_and_WTC_fire.jpg/250px-National_Park_Service_9-11_Statue_of_Liberty_and_WTC_fire.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Park_Service_9-11_Statue_of_Liberty_and_WTC_fire.jpg)

September 11 attacks in New York City: View of the World Trade Center and the Statue of Liberty. (Image: US National Park Service)

In the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") on September 11, 2001, 19 [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") hijackers commandeered four commercial planes to be used in [suicide attacks](/wiki/Suicide_attack "Suicide attack"). Two were crashed intentionally into both Twin Towers of the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and a third into [the Pentagon](/wiki/The_Pentagon "The Pentagon") in [Arlington County, Virginia](/wiki/Arlington_County,_Virginia "Arlington County, Virginia"). The [fourth plane](/wiki/United_Airlines_Flight_93 "United Airlines Flight 93") was retaken by the passengers and crew and crashed into an [empty field](/wiki/Stonycreek_Township,_Somerset_County,_Pennsylvania "Stonycreek Township, Somerset County, Pennsylvania") in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). Every building of the World Trade Center partially or completely [collapsed](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_World_Trade_Center "Collapse of the World Trade Center"), massively damaging the surrounding area and blanketing Lower Manhattan in [toxic dust clouds](/wiki/Health_effects_arising_from_the_September_11_attacks "Health effects arising from the September 11 attacks"). 2,977 victims died in the attacks, which proved the deadliest terrorist attack in world history.[[300]](#cite_note-statista.com-304)

On September 20, Bush announced a "[war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror")".[[301]](#cite_note-305)[[302]](#cite_note-306) In October 2001, the U.S. and NATO [invaded Afghanistan](/wiki/United_States_invasion_of_Afghanistan "United States invasion of Afghanistan") and ousted the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban") regime, which had harbored al-Qaeda and its leader [Osama bin Laden](/wiki/Osama_bin_Laden "Osama bin Laden").[[303]](#cite_note-307) Bin Laden escaped to Pakistan, starting a [manhunt](/wiki/Manhunt_for_Osama_bin_Laden "Manhunt for Osama bin Laden").[[304]](#cite_note-308) The U.S. established new domestic efforts to prevent future attacks. The [Patriot Act](/wiki/Patriot_Act "Patriot Act") increased the power of government to monitor communications and removed legal restrictions on intelligence sharing between federal law enforcement agencies.[[305]](#cite_note-Zelizer-2010b-309) The government's [indefinite detention](/wiki/Indefinite_detention "Indefinite detention") of terrorism suspects captured abroad at the [Guantanamo Bay detention camp](/wiki/Guantanamo_Bay_detention_camp "Guantanamo Bay detention camp") led to allegations of human rights abuses and violations of international law.[[306]](#cite_note-310)[[307]](#cite_note-311)[[308]](#cite_note-312) The [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/Department_of_Homeland_Security "Department of Homeland Security") was created to lead federal counter-terrorism activities.[[305]](#cite_note-Zelizer-2010b-309)

In March 2003, the U.S. launched [an invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq"), claiming Iraqi dictator [Saddam Hussein](/wiki/Saddam_Hussein "Saddam Hussein") had [weapons of mass destruction](/wiki/Weapons_of_mass_destruction "Weapons of mass destruction") (WMDs). Intelligence backing WMDs were later found to be inaccurate. The war led to the collapse of the Iraqi government and the eventual [capture](/wiki/Capture_of_Saddam_Hussein "Capture of Saddam Hussein") of Hussein.[[309]](#cite_note-313)[[310]](#cite_note-314) The [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War") fueled [international protests](/wiki/Protests_against_the_Iraq_War "Protests against the Iraq War") and gradually saw [domestic support decline](/wiki/Public_opinion_in_the_United_States_on_the_invasion_of_Iraq "Public opinion in the United States on the invasion of Iraq").[[311]](#cite_note-315)[[312]](#cite_note-316)

In 2005, [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina") killed 1,800 people around New Orleans after the city's [levees](/wiki/Levee "Levee") broke.[[313]](#cite_note-317) In 2007, after years of violence by the [Iraqi insurgency](/wiki/Iraqi_insurgency_(2003%E2%80%932011) "Iraqi insurgency (2003–2011)"), Bush deployed more troops in a strategy dubbed "[the surge](/wiki/Iraq_War_troop_surge_of_2007 "Iraq War troop surge of 2007")". While the death toll decreased, the political stability of Iraq remained in doubt.[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz453-318) In 2008, the U.S. entered the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession_in_the_United_States "Great Recession in the United States").[[315]](#cite_note-319)[[316]](#cite_note-320) Multiple overlapping crises were involved, especially the [housing market crisis](/wiki/United_States_housing_market_correction "United States housing market correction"), a [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis"), [soaring oil prices](/wiki/2000s_energy_crisis "2000s energy crisis"), an [automotive industry crisis](/wiki/Effects_of_the_2008%E2%80%932010_automotive_industry_crisis_on_the_United_States "Effects of the 2008–2010 automotive industry crisis on the United States"), rising unemployment, and the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), the worst [financial crisis](/wiki/Financial_crisis "Financial crisis") since the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). The [bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Bankruptcy_of_Lehman_Brothers "Bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers") threatened the stability of the entire economy in September 2008.[[317]](#cite_note-321) Starting in October, the federal government lent $245 billion to financial institutions through the bipartisan [Troubled Asset Relief Program](/wiki/Troubled_Asset_Relief_Program "Troubled Asset Relief Program").[[318]](#cite_note-322)[[319]](#cite_note-323)

### Obama administration

Main articles: [Great Recession in the United States](/wiki/Great_Recession_in_the_United_States "Great Recession in the United States"), [Presidency of Barack Obama](/wiki/Presidency_of_Barack_Obama "Presidency of Barack Obama"), and [Tea Party movement](/wiki/Tea_Party_movement "Tea Party movement")

[Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") delivering his [2009 inauguration](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Barack_Obama "First inauguration of Barack Obama") speech

[Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), the first multiracial and African American[[320]](#cite_note-324) president, was [elected in 2008](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election").[[321]](#cite_note-325) He signed the [Don't Ask, Don't Tell Repeal Act](/wiki/Don%27t_Ask,_Don%27t_Tell_Repeal_Act "Don't Ask, Don't Tell Repeal Act"), which allowed people to serve in the military while openly gay.[[322]](#cite_note-326) To help the economy, he signed the [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009"),[[323]](#cite_note-327) [Consumer Assistance to Recycle and Save Act](/wiki/Car_Allowance_Rebate_System "Car Allowance Rebate System"),[[324]](#cite_note-328) the [Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Patient_Protection_and_Affordable_Care_Act "Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act"), informally called "Obamacare",[[325]](#cite_note-329) and the [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act").[[326]](#cite_note-330)[[327]](#cite_note-331) The unemployment rate began falling as the economy and labor markets experienced a recovery.[[328]](#cite_note-MLR-2018-332)

These changes to the economic system created new political movements, such as the [Occupy movement](/wiki/Occupy_movement "Occupy movement") and the [Tea Party movement](/wiki/Tea_Party_movement "Tea Party movement").[[329]](#cite_note-333) The recession officially ended in mid-2009.[[330]](#cite_note-334) Following the [2010 midterm elections](/wiki/2010_midterm_elections "2010 midterm elections"), Congress was in [gridlock](/wiki/Gridlock_(politics) "Gridlock (politics)"),[[331]](#cite_note-335) leading to the [Budget Control Act of 2011](/wiki/Budget_Control_Act_of_2011 "Budget Control Act of 2011").[[332]](#cite_note-336) The economic expansion that followed the Great Recession was the longest in U.S. history;[[333]](#cite_note-Rushe-337)[[334]](#cite_note-Bayly-338) the unemployment rate reached a 50-year low in 2019.[[335]](#cite_note-339) Despite the strong economy, increases in the cost of living surpassed increases in wages.[[336]](#cite_note-340)[[337]](#cite_note-341)

In 2009, Obama issued an [executive order banning the use of torture](/wiki/Executive_Order_13491 "Executive Order 13491").[[338]](#cite_note-342)[[339]](#cite_note-Barela-343) He ordered the closure of [secret CIA-run prisons](/wiki/CIA_black_sites "CIA black sites") overseas,[[340]](#cite_note-344)[[341]](#cite_note-345) and [sought to close](/wiki/Executive_Order_13492 "Executive Order 13492") the Guantanamo Bay detention camp, but his efforts were stymied by Congress.[[342]](#cite_note-Rath-346)[[343]](#cite_note-347) American military personnel [left Iraq](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_U.S._troops_from_Iraq_(2007%E2%80%932011) "Withdrawal of U.S. troops from Iraq (2007–2011)") in 2011.[[344]](#cite_note-348) Meanwhile, Obama increased involvement in Afghanistan, adding 30,000 troops, while proposing to begin [withdrawal](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_U.S._troops_from_Afghanistan_(2011%E2%80%932016) "Withdrawal of U.S. troops from Afghanistan (2011–2016)") in 2014.[[339]](#cite_note-Barela-343) The U.S., with NATO, [intervened](/wiki/2011_military_intervention_in_Libya "2011 military intervention in Libya") in the [Libyan Civil War](/wiki/Libyan_civil_war_(2011) "Libyan civil war (2011)") in 2011.[[345]](#cite_note-349) In May 2011, Osama bin Laden [was killed](/wiki/Killing_of_Osama_bin_Laden "Killing of Osama bin Laden") in Pakistan in a [Navy SEALs](/wiki/Navy_SEALs "Navy SEALs") raid ordered by Obama. While al-Qaeda was near collapse in Afghanistan, affiliated organizations continued to operate in Yemen and other remote areas, as the CIA used [drones](/wiki/Unmanned_combat_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned combat aerial vehicle") to hunt down its leadership.[[346]](#cite_note-350)[[347]](#cite_note-351) In October, Obama [sent troops to Central Africa](/wiki/Operation_Observant_Compass "Operation Observant Compass") to fight the [Lord's Resistance Army](/wiki/Lord%27s_Resistance_Army "Lord's Resistance Army").[[348]](#cite_note-352)

Following Obama's [2012 re-election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election"), Congressional gridlock continued, resulting in the [first government shutdown since the Clinton administration](/wiki/2013_United_States_federal_government_shutdown "2013 United States federal government shutdown").[[349]](#cite_note-353) In 2012, the [Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting](/wiki/Sandy_Hook_Elementary_School_shooting "Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting") in [Newtown, Connecticut](/wiki/Newtown,_Connecticut "Newtown, Connecticut"), led to unsuccessful attempts from Obama to promote [gun reform](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_the_United_States "Gun politics in the United States").[[350]](#cite_note-354) The [Boston Marathon bombing](/wiki/Boston_Marathon_bombing "Boston Marathon bombing") of 2013 killed three people and injured more than 260.[[351]](#cite_note-355) In 2013, the U.S. also started a [counter-terrorist intervention](/wiki/US_military_intervention_in_Niger "US military intervention in Niger") in Niger,[[352]](#cite_note-356) and began a covert operation to [train rebels in Syria](/wiki/Syrian_Train_and_Equip_Program "Syrian Train and Equip Program") who were fighting against the terrorist group [ISIS](/wiki/ISIS "ISIS"). The latter program was publicized and [expanded](/wiki/US_intervention_in_the_Syrian_civil_war "US intervention in the Syrian civil war") in 2014.[[353]](#cite_note-357) That year, ISIS grew in scope in the Middle East, and inspired many terrorist attacks in the United States, including the [2015 San Bernardino attack](/wiki/2015_San_Bernardino_attack "2015 San Bernardino attack").[[354]](#cite_note-358)[[355]](#cite_note-359)[[356]](#cite_note-360) The U.S. and its allies began a significant [military offensive against ISIS](/wiki/US-led_intervention_in_Iraq_(2014%E2%80%932021) "US-led intervention in Iraq (2014–2021)") in Iraq which lasted from 2014 to 2021.[[357]](#cite_note-361)[[358]](#cite_note-362) In December 2014, Obama officially ended the combat mission in Afghanistan.[[359]](#cite_note-363)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/COP21_participants_-_30_Nov_2015_%2823430273715%29.jpg/250px-COP21_participants_-_30_Nov_2015_%2823430273715%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:COP21_participants_-_30_Nov_2015_(23430273715).jpg)

Heads of delegations at the [2015 United Nations Climate Change Conference](/wiki/2015_United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference "2015 United Nations Climate Change Conference") (COP21), which led to the signing of the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement").

The [shooting](/wiki/Killing_of_Michael_Brown "Killing of Michael Brown") of Black teen Michael Brown by police officer Darren Wilson, and a grand jury declining to charge Wilson with murder, led to the [Ferguson unrest](/wiki/Ferguson_unrest "Ferguson unrest") in Missouri in 2014 and 2015.[[360]](#cite_note-364) In 2012, President Obama became the first president to openly support same-sex marriage.[[361]](#cite_note-365) The Supreme Court provided [federal recognition of same-sex marriages](/wiki/United_States_v._Windsor "United States v. Windsor") in 2013,[[362]](#cite_note-366) and then legalized gay marriage nationwide with *[Obergefell v. Hodges](/wiki/Obergefell_v._Hodges "Obergefell v. Hodges")* in 2015.[[363]](#cite_note-367) Also in 2015, the U.S. joined the international [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") on [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[364]](#cite_note-Reuters-2020-368)

### First Trump administration

Main articles: [First presidency of Donald Trump](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump") and [COVID-19 pandemic in the United States](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Park_signs_in_West_Seattle_during_the_COVID-19_outbreak_03_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Park_signs_in_West_Seattle_during_the_COVID-19_outbreak_03_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Park_signs_in_West_Seattle_during_the_COVID-19_outbreak_03_(cropped).jpg)

A sign emphasizing [social distancing](/wiki/Social_distancing "Social distancing") displayed in a park in Seattle, Washington during the [COVID-19 Pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic")

[After decades of testing the political waters](/wiki/Political_career_of_Donald_Trump "Political career of Donald Trump"), punctuated by a series of events like espousing the [Birther Movement](/wiki/Barack_Obama_citizenship_conspiracy_theories "Barack Obama citizenship conspiracy theories"), President Obama's scathing response to it at the [2011 White House Correspondents Dinner](/wiki/White_House_Correspondents_Dinner "White House Correspondents Dinner"), and finally endorsing the failed campaign of [Mitt Romney in 2012](/wiki/Mitt_Romney_2012_presidential_campaign "Mitt Romney 2012 presidential campaign"). [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") became the Republican Party nominee and won the 2016 election in an upset victory over Hillary Clinton.[[365]](#cite_note-369) The election's legitimacy was disputed when [the FBI](/wiki/Crossfire_Hurricane_(FBI_investigation) "Crossfire Hurricane (FBI investigation)") and [Congress](/wiki/Senate_Intelligence_Committee_report_on_Russian_interference_in_the_2016_United_States_presidential_election "Senate Intelligence Committee report on Russian interference in the 2016 United States presidential election") investigated if Russia [interfered in the election](/wiki/Russian_interference_in_the_2016_United_States_elections "Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections") to help Trump win. There were also [accusations of collusion](/wiki/Links_between_Trump_associates_and_Russian_officials "Links between Trump associates and Russian officials") between Trump's campaign and Russian officials. The [Mueller report](/wiki/Mueller_report "Mueller report") concluded that Russia attempted to help Trump's campaign, but there was no evidence of "explicit" collusion.[[366]](#cite_note-370)[[367]](#cite_note-AP_News-2023-371)[[368]](#cite_note-372)

During Trump's presidency, he espoused an "[America First](/wiki/America_First_(policy) "America First (policy)")" ideology, placing restrictions on asylum seekers, [expanding the wall](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_wall "Mexico–United States border wall") on the U.S.-Mexico border, and [banning immigration from seven Muslim-majority countries](/wiki/Trump_travel_ban "Trump travel ban"). Many of his actions were challenged in court.[[369]](#cite_note-373)[[370]](#cite_note-374)[[371]](#cite_note-375) He confirmed [three new Supreme Court justices](/wiki/Donald_Trump_Supreme_Court_candidates "Donald Trump Supreme Court candidates") (cementing a conservative majority),[[372]](#cite_note-376) started a [trade war with China](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war"),[[373]](#cite_note-377) signed the [Tax Cuts and Jobs Act](/wiki/Tax_Cuts_and_Jobs_Act "Tax Cuts and Jobs Act"), and removed the U.S. from the Paris Agreement.[[364]](#cite_note-Reuters-2020-368) In 2018, the administration [separated families](/wiki/Trump_administration_family_separation_policy "Trump administration family separation policy") which were illegally immigrating to the country. After public outcry, Trump rescinded the policy.[[374]](#cite_note-378)[[375]](#cite_note-379) A whistleblower complaint alleged that Trump had [withheld foreign aid from Ukraine](/wiki/Trump%E2%80%93Ukraine_scandal "Trump–Ukraine scandal") under the demand that they investigate the business dealings of [Hunter Biden](/wiki/Hunter_Biden "Hunter Biden"); Hunter's father, Democrat [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden"), would be Trump's opponent in the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election").[[376]](#cite_note-380)[[377]](#cite_note-Gilliland-2020-381) Trump was [impeached](/wiki/First_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "First impeachment of Donald Trump") for abuse of power and obstruction of congress, but [was acquitted](/wiki/First_impeachment_trial_of_Donald_Trump "First impeachment trial of Donald Trump") in 2020.[[378]](#cite_note-382)

In the 2010s and early 2020s, Americans became more [politically polarized](/wiki/Political_polarization_in_the_United_States "Political polarization in the United States").[[379]](#cite_note-383)[[380]](#cite_note-384)[[381]](#cite_note-385) The [#MeToo](/wiki/Me_Too_movement "Me Too movement") movement exposed alleged sexual harassment and abuse in the workplace.[[382]](#cite_note-386) Many celebrities were accused of misconduct or rape.[[383]](#cite_note-387)[[384]](#cite_note-388) The [Black Lives Matter](/wiki/Black_Lives_Matter "Black Lives Matter") movement gained support after multiple police killings of African-Americans.[[385]](#cite_note-389) White supremacy also grew.[[386]](#cite_note-390)[[387]](#cite_note-391)[[388]](#cite_note-392) The [2017 Women's March](/wiki/2017_Women%27s_March "2017 Women's March") against Trump's presidency was one of the largest protests in American history.[[389]](#cite_note-393) Multiple mass shootings, including the 2016 [Pulse Nightclub shooting](/wiki/Orlando_nightclub_shooting "Orlando nightclub shooting"), 2017 [Las Vegas shooting](/wiki/Las_Vegas_shooting "Las Vegas shooting"), and 2018 [Parkland shooting](/wiki/Parkland_shooting "Parkland shooting"), led to increased calls for gun reform, such as in the [March for Our Lives](/wiki/March_for_Our_Lives "March for Our Lives") student protest movement.[[390]](#cite_note-394)[[391]](#cite_note-395)

[COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") started spreading in China in 2019.[[392]](#cite_note-396) In March 2020, the [WHO](/wiki/WHO "WHO") declared a [pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[393]](#cite_note-397) American state and local governments imposed [stay-at-home orders](/wiki/Stay-at-home_order "Stay-at-home order") to slow the virus' spread, [reducing patient overload](/wiki/Flattening_the_curve "Flattening the curve") in hospitals. By April, the U.S. had the most cases of any country, at 100,000.[[394]](#cite_note-398)[[395]](#cite_note-399)[[396]](#cite_note-400) On April 11, the U.S. death toll became the highest in the world at 20,000,[[397]](#cite_note-401) and by May 2022, one million had died.[[398]](#cite_note-402) Unemployment rates were the highest since the Great Depression.[[399]](#cite_note-403)[[400]](#cite_note-404) The [biggest mass vaccination campaign in U.S. history](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccination_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 vaccination in the United States") started in December 2020.[[401]](#cite_note-405)
The May 2020 [murder of George Floyd](/wiki/Murder_of_George_Floyd "Murder of George Floyd") caused mass [protests and riots](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests "George Floyd protests") in many cities over police brutality.[[402]](#cite_note-406) Many organizations attempted to rid themselves of institutionalized racism.[[403]](#cite_note-407) 2020 was also marked by a rise in domestic terrorist threats and widespread [conspiracy theories](/wiki/Conspiracy_theories "Conspiracy theories") around [mail-in voting](/wiki/Postal_voting_in_the_2020_United_States_elections "Postal voting in the 2020 United States elections") and COVID-19.[[404]](#cite_note-408)[[405]](#cite_note-409)[[406]](#cite_note-410) The [QAnon](/wiki/QAnon "QAnon") conspiracy theory gained publicity.[[407]](#cite_note-411)[[408]](#cite_note-412) Multiple major cities were hit by rioting and fighting between far-left [anti-fascist groups](/wiki/Antifa_(United_States) "Antifa (United States)") and far-right groups like the [Proud Boys](/wiki/Proud_Boys "Proud Boys").[[409]](#cite_note-413)[[410]](#cite_note-414)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/2021_storming_of_the_United_States_Capitol_DSC09254-2_%2850820534063%29_%28retouched%29.jpg/250px-2021_storming_of_the_United_States_Capitol_DSC09254-2_%2850820534063%29_%28retouched%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2021_storming_of_the_United_States_Capitol_DSC09254-2_(50820534063)_(retouched).jpg)

Supporters of then-[President Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") attempting to stop the counting of [electoral votes](/wiki/Electoral_votes "Electoral votes") on January 6, 2021

Joe Biden defeated Trump in the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election").[[377]](#cite_note-Gilliland-2020-381) Trump repeatedly made [false claims](/wiki/Election_denial_movement_in_the_United_States "Election denial movement in the United States") of massive voter fraud and election rigging,[[411]](#cite_note-415)[[412]](#cite_note-416)[[413]](#cite_note-417) leading to the [January 6 United States Capitol attack](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack") by supporters of Trump and right-wing militias.[[414]](#cite_note-418)[[415]](#cite_note-419) The attack was widely described as a [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état").[[416]](#cite_note-420)[[417]](#cite_note-421)[[418]](#cite_note-Multiple_Sources-422) It led to Trump's [impeachment](/wiki/Second_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "Second impeachment of Donald Trump") for incitement of insurrection, making him the only U.S. president to be impeached twice.[[419]](#cite_note-423)[[420]](#cite_note-424)[[421]](#cite_note-425) The Senate later [acquitted](/wiki/Second_impeachment_trial_of_Donald_Trump "Second impeachment trial of Donald Trump") Trump, despite some fellow Republicans voting against him.[[422]](#cite_note-426)[[423]](#cite_note-427) [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") was [inaugurated](/wiki/Inauguration_of_Joe_Biden "Inauguration of Joe Biden") as the first Black, Asian, and female vice president.[[424]](#cite_note-428)

### Biden administration

Main article: [Presidency of Joe Biden](/wiki/Presidency_of_Joe_Biden "Presidency of Joe Biden")

In 2021, Biden completed the [withdrawal of American troops](/wiki/2020%E2%80%932021_U.S._troop_withdrawal_from_Afghanistan "2020–2021 U.S. troop withdrawal from Afghanistan") from Afghanistan which had started under the first Trump administration. After an [evacuation of over 120,000 American citizens](/wiki/2021_Kabul_airlift "2021 Kabul airlift"), Afghanistan [fell to the Taliban](/wiki/Fall_of_Afghanistan "Fall of Afghanistan") in August.[[425]](#cite_note-AP_News-2022-429)[[426]](#cite_note-430)[[427]](#cite_note-431) Biden signed into law the [American Rescue Plan Act of 2021](/wiki/American_Rescue_Plan_Act_of_2021 "American Rescue Plan Act of 2021"), a $1.9 trillion stimulus bill.[[428]](#cite_note-432) He also proposed a significant expansion of the social safety net through the [Build Back Better Act](/wiki/Build_Back_Better_Act "Build Back Better Act"), but those efforts, along with [voting rights legislation](/wiki/Freedom_to_Vote_Act "Freedom to Vote Act"), failed in Congress.[[429]](#cite_note-433) He signed bills regarding [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure_Investment_and_Jobs_Act "Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act"),[[430]](#cite_note-434) [gun reform](/wiki/Bipartisan_Safer_Communities_Act "Bipartisan Safer Communities Act"),[[431]](#cite_note-435) [inflation reduction](/wiki/Inflation_Reduction_Act "Inflation Reduction Act"),[[432]](#cite_note-436) and [healthcare for veterans](/wiki/Honoring_our_PACT_Act_of_2022 "Honoring our PACT Act of 2022"),[[433]](#cite_note-437) among other issues.[[434]](#cite_note-438) New preventative restrictions were put in place in reaction to the [SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Delta_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant").[[435]](#cite_note-439)[[436]](#cite_note-440)[[437]](#cite_note-441)

In 2022, following [Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), the Biden administration provided extensive military and economic aid to Ukraine, approving tens of billions of dollars in assistance and coordinating sanctions against Russia with NATO allies.[[438]](#cite_note-442) The U.S. also supplied advanced weaponry, including artillery and missile defense systems, while reinforcing NATO's eastern flank in response to the conflict.[[439]](#cite_note-443)[[440]](#cite_note-444)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/-i---i-_%2852380995447%29.jpg/250px--i---i-_%2852380995447%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:-i---i-_(52380995447).jpg)

Protestors outside the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") following the *[Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization](/wiki/Dobbs_v._Jackson_Women%27s_Health_Organization "Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization")* decision in June 2022

In the early 2020s, Republican-led states began [rollbacks of LGBTQ rights](/wiki/2020s_anti-LGBTQ_movement_in_the_United_States "2020s anti-LGBTQ movement in the United States") as well as [restrictions on voting rights](/wiki/Republican_efforts_to_restrict_voting_following_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Republican efforts to restrict voting following the 2020 United States presidential election").[[441]](#cite_note-445) In 2022, the Supreme Court ruled in *[Dobbs v. Jackson](/wiki/Dobbs_v._Jackson "Dobbs v. Jackson")* that the right to have an abortion is not protected by the U.S. Constitution, overturning *Roe v. Wade* and *[Planned Parenthood v. Casey](/wiki/Planned_Parenthood_v._Casey "Planned Parenthood v. Casey")* and sparking [nationwide protests](/wiki/2022_abortion_rights_protests_in_the_United_States "2022 abortion rights protests in the United States").[[442]](#cite_note-446)[[443]](#cite_note-447) Biden appointed [Ketanji Brown Jackson](/wiki/Ketanji_Brown_Jackson "Ketanji Brown Jackson") to become the first Black woman to serve on the court.[[444]](#cite_note-448) Meanwhile, the U.S. began supporting Israel in the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war")[[445]](#cite_note-449) and [protecting shipping](/wiki/Operation_Prosperity_Guardian "Operation Prosperity Guardian") in the Red Sea from attacks by the Yemeni [Houthis](/wiki/Houthis "Houthis").[[446]](#cite_note-450)

In May 2024, Trump became the first former president to be convicted of a crime, when found guilty of [34 felony counts](/wiki/Prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_in_New_York "Prosecution of Donald Trump in New York") for falsifying business documents related to his [paying off of Stormy Daniels](/wiki/Stormy_Daniels%E2%80%93Donald_Trump_scandal "Stormy Daniels–Donald Trump scandal").[[447]](#cite_note-451) In July, the Supreme Court ruled in *[Trump v. United States](/wiki/Trump_v._United_States_(2024) "Trump v. United States (2024)")* that presidents are somewhat [immune from criminal prosecution](/wiki/Presidential_immunity_in_the_United_States "Presidential immunity in the United States"). Later in July, Biden [dropped out](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_Joe_Biden_from_the_2024_United_States_presidential_election "Withdrawal of Joe Biden from the 2024 United States presidential election") of the 2024 race and endorsed his running mate Kamala Harris for president.[[448]](#cite_note-452) During the election season, there were [two assassination attempts](/wiki/List_of_United_States_presidential_assassination_attempts_and_plots#Donald_Trump "List of United States presidential assassination attempts and plots") on Trump.[[449]](#cite_note-453) Trump won the 2024 presidential election.[[450]](#cite_note-454)[[451]](#cite_note-455) Biden delivered [his farewell address](/wiki/Joe_Biden%27s_farewell_address "Joe Biden's farewell address") from the Oval Office on January 15, 2025. He opened by announcing that a [hostage release deal](/wiki/Gaza_war_hostage_crisis "Gaza war hostage crisis") had been reached between Israel and Hamas. Additionally, he advocated for continued renewable energy investment, strengthening [checks and balances](/wiki/Checks_and_balances "Checks and balances") in government, and warned against the dangers of what he termed the '[tech–industrial complex](/wiki/Tech%E2%80%93industrial_complex "Tech–industrial complex")'.[[452]](#cite_note-456)

### Second Trump administration

Main article: [Second presidency of Donald Trump](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Uncle_Sam_Straddles_the_Americas_Cartoon.jpg/250px-Uncle_Sam_Straddles_the_Americas_Cartoon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uncle_Sam_Straddles_the_Americas_Cartoon.jpg)

The [Monroe Doctrine](/wiki/Monroe_Doctrine "Monroe Doctrine") featured prominently as the primary foreign policy of the administration

#### Domestic affairs

In November 2024, Trump was elected president to a nonconsecutive second term, only the second time in U.S. history for such a scenario to occur, the first being [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland").[[453]](#cite_note-457)[[454]](#cite_note-458) On his first day, Trump [pardoned about 1,500 people](/wiki/Pardon_of_January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack_defendants "Pardon of January 6 United States Capitol attack defendants") convicted of offenses in the [January 6 Capitol attack](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack") of 2021. Within his first month, he signed approximately 70 [executive orders](/wiki/List_of_executive_orders_in_the_second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "List of executive orders in the second presidency of Donald Trump") (far more than any of his recent predecessors), some of which are being [challenged in court](/wiki/Court_cases_related_to_Donald_Trump%27s_second_presidential_term "Court cases related to Donald Trump's second presidential term").[[455]](#cite_note-459) On immigration, the [Laken Riley Act](/wiki/Laken_Riley_Act "Laken Riley Act") was passed as the first legislation of his term as well as executive orders blocking asylum-seekers from entry to the U.S. He also reinstated [the national emergency](/wiki/National_Emergency_Concerning_the_Southern_Border_of_the_United_States "National Emergency Concerning the Southern Border of the United States") at the [Mexico–U.S. border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"), designated drug cartels as terrorist organizations, and attempted to end [birthright citizenship](/wiki/Birthright_citizenship_in_the_United_States "Birthright citizenship in the United States"). Trump established the [Department of Government Efficiency](/wiki/Department_of_Government_Efficiency "Department of Government Efficiency") (DOGE), briefly led by [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk"), which was tasked with cutting spending by [the federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"), limiting bureaucracy, and which oversaw [mass layoffs of civil servants](/wiki/2025_United_States_federal_mass_layoffs "2025 United States federal mass layoffs") before disbanding in late 2025.

With passage of the [OBBBA](/wiki/One_Big_Beautiful_Bill_Act "One Big Beautiful Bill Act") in mid-2025, the administration allocated funding to carry out most of its planned core domestic policy, including more than 1,000 miles of [southern border wall construction](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_wall#Second_Trump_administration_(2025–present) "Mexico–United States border wall"), making permanent the [2017 TCJA](/wiki/Tax_Cuts_and_Jobs_Act "Tax Cuts and Jobs Act") tax cuts, and a historic increase in [ICE's budget](/wiki/United_States_Immigration_and_Customs_Enforcement "United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement") to effectuate what the administration promised would be the largest deportation program since the Eisenhower era.[[456]](#cite_note-460)

#### Foreign affairs

In international affairs, Trump withdrew the United States from the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") and the [Paris Climate Accords](/wiki/Paris_Climate_Accords "Paris Climate Accords"). He started a [trade war with Canada and Mexico](/wiki/Second_Trump_tariffs "Second Trump tariffs") and continued the ongoing [trade war with China](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war").

Amid the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), the Trump administration engaged in multiple rounds of peace negotiations with both sides, the negotiations had limited progress as the war reached its 4-year anniversary with little sign of abatement.[[457]](#cite_note-461)[[458]](#cite_note-462)

In response to the [Gaza War](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"), the administration brokered a peace deal that led to the [United Nations Security Council Resolution 2803](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_2803 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 2803") and the formation of a [Gaza Board of Peace](/wiki/Board_of_Peace "Board of Peace"), designed to administrate the recovery and reconstruction of the Gaza Strip.

In Iran, in mid-2025 the administration neutralized [alleged sites where Iran was enriching weapon grade uranium](/wiki/Operation_Midnight_Hammer "Operation Midnight Hammer"), [then entered diplomatic negotiations](/wiki/2025%E2%80%932026_Iran%E2%80%93United_States_negotiations#2026_negotiations "2025–2026 Iran–United States negotiations") in hopes for a long-term arms control and nuclear treaty with Iran. [Mass protests occurred in Iran](/wiki/2025%E2%80%932026_Iranian_protests "2025–2026 Iranian protests") with the regime reportedly killing as many as 35,000 dissenters. Negotiations failed and after the largest buildup of US armed forces in decades in the middle east, on February 28, 2026, the long-time leader of Iran, [Ali Khomeini was assassinated at his compound](/wiki/Assassination_of_Ali_Khamenei "Assassination of Ali Khamenei"). The strike became the opening salvo of the [2026 Iran War](/wiki/2026_Iran_war "2026 Iran war").[[459]](#cite_note-463)

Trump invoked the [Monroe Doctrine](/wiki/Monroe_Doctrine "Monroe Doctrine"), a foreign policy position first expressed by 5th President [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe"), which asserts that the United States has broad authority to take actions in the interest of its national security within the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"), to justify the capture of Venezuelan President [Nicholas Maduro](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Maduro "Nicolás Maduro") in [Operation Absolute Resolve](/wiki/Operation_Absolute_Resolve "Operation Absolute Resolve")[[460]](#cite_note-464) and to constrain alleged undue Chinese owned firm influence in the operation of the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal").[[461]](#cite_note-465)

Since the beginning of his term, the Trump administration has threatened American withdrawal from [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), despite the highly significant role of the United States in the alliance, alongside its threats to the sovereignty of two NATO-founding countries, [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and the [Kingdom of Denmark](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Denmark "Kingdom of Denmark"), triggering an unprecedented NATO crisis. Combined with its [trade war with the European Union](/wiki/US-EU_trade_war_(Greenland_crisis) "US-EU trade war (Greenland crisis)"), and particularly Denmark's position as a European state, decades of [Euro–American relations](/wiki/United_States%E2%80%93European_Union_relations "United States–European Union relations") have been "undone".[[462]](#cite_note-466)[[463]](#cite_note-467)
